His partner Gina, his former lover, is forced to kill him at his own request - he wants her to shoot him in his tongue, the only part of his body that reminds him he's still human.

The series was famously halted after just three episodes due to a legal dispute between production company Artmic and Youmex, leaving the Bubblegum Crisis timeline permanently fragmented.
entries.core:
AD Police Files is a unflinching examination of transhumanism and identity crisis in the cyberpunk genre. The series poses uncomfortable questions: At what point does technology strip us of our humanity? The "Boomeroid" law - where 70% cybernetic modification makes you legally killable - is a chilling metaphor for how society categorizes and dehumanizes those who are different.

The three episodes form a thematic trilogy. Episode 1 asks whether artificial beings can possess authentic emotions and desires. Episode 2 explores how technology can both empower and dehumanize, as Caroline Evers literally becomes a monster despite her human consciousness. Episode 3 is the most devastating - Billy's tragedy shows that even a hero's "human" spirit can be systematically destroyed by those who claim to be helping him.

The series predates and influenced later cyberpunk works like Ghost in the Shell, yet remains largely forgotten outside of die-hard anime collectors. Its dark, noir atmosphere and willingness to engage with uncomfortable philosophical questions about consciousness, identity, and what makes us human elevates it far above typical 80s OVA fare.

entries.title: Accumulator 1
entries.creator: Jan Svěrák (Director), Zdeněk Svěrák (Writer)
entries.pitch: Accumulator 1 is a 1994 Czech surrealist science fiction film directed by Jan Svěrák (his debut feature), with co-writing by his father Zdeněk Svěrák. The film won the Grand Prize at the 7th Yubari International Fantastic Film Festival in February 1996 and the audience award at the Tromsø International Film Festival in 1996. The story follows Young surveyor Olda, who after losing consciousness for three days, discovers that television sets secretly drain people's life energy. When he is pulled into a parallel "television universe" inhabited by doppelgangers of people who have appeared on TV, Olda must find a way to destroy this parasitic realm before it consumes all of humanity. The film is a surreal, visually inventive exploration of media consumption, energy vampirism, and the alienating effects of television on modern life.
entries.truth:
The film opens with Olda (Petr Forman), a young surveyor, whose girlfriend Jitka (Tereza Pergnerová) is seduced by his colleague Slezák (Bolek Polívka). Olda does nothing to stop them, instead watching television all night, including an absurd interview he participated in. He subsequently loses consciousness for three days and awakens in a hospital room with the ageing Mikulík (Jiří Kodet).

That night, a mysterious healer named Fišarek (Zdeněk Svěrák) appears and teaches Olda how to replenish his lost energy by drawing it from trees, works of art, and natural phenomena. When Mikulik dies—having lost all his energy from watching TV—they examine his body and meet his daughter Anna (Edita Brychta).

Fišarek continues his healing experiments on Olda with a group of mediums. One day, they see an old teacher on television, and the action shifts into the television universe itself—a surreal平行 world inhabited by doppelgangers of people who have appeared on TV. These television alter-egos live a hedonistic party lifestyle, shamelessly feeding on energy from their real-world counterparts.

Olda eventually finds Anna in a phone book and takes her on a date. However, when he accidentally turns on a television, it steals his energy again. He meets the teacher from the TV show, who has experienced the same fate. The teacher flees to a remote village without television, while Olda remains in Prague to be close to Anna.

Olda proposes marriage to Anna, but she reveals she has a daughter. He buys remote controls as protection against TV sets. In a power cut, Olda reads Anna's daughter a fairy tale about a prince who defeated a witch by flooding her with life force from several people. He formulates a similar plan to destroy the television "mirror world" by oversaturating a TV set with energy.

Meanwhile, in the television world, "dead" media personalities are symbolically buried, lowered from a ship into a landfill. Most will lie down and talk because their originals died, but Olda's tele-ego cannot drain energy because his original knows about the situation and refuses to allow it.

The next day, Olda gathers as much energy as possible from people in the city and attempts his plan that night. He is interrupted by Slezák, who is drunk and looking for Jitka. Olda detonates the charged boards in his apartment, sending released energy across the bridge toward the television. At the last moment, he abandons the plan, realizing he is standing in the path of the stolen energy. He releases the energy, which instead saves a falling plane.

As the dust settles, Olda's tele-ego appears, and they embrace and merge together. Slezák, who broke into the apartment before the explosion, is arrested. The next morning, Olda calls Anna to apologize. Television is no longer dangerous for him personally, but the global problem remains unsolved.
entries.core:
**Television as Parasitic Force:** The film's central metaphor treats television as a vampiric force that drains life energy from viewers. This is visualized through the surreal "television universe" where television alter-egos live parasitically off their real-world counterparts. The film critiques mass media as an exploitative entity that consumes the viewer's time, attention, and ultimately their life force—a prescient commentary on passive media consumption that resonates even more strongly in the age of streaming and social media.

**Energy and Capitalism:** The film can be read as an allegory for economic exploitation. Just as the television universe extracts energy from real people without giving anything back, the film suggests a metaphor for how systems extract value from individuals. The character of Fišarek, who teaches Olda to draw energy from positive sources (trees, art, nature), represents an alternative to this parasitic relationship—one based on genuine connection rather than extraction.

**Surrealism and Social Critique:** Following in the tradition of Czech surrealism (including filmmakers like Jan Švankmajer), Svěrák employs dreamlike imagery and surrealist logic to critique modern society. The television universe, with its hedonistic excess and disconnection from reality, represents a dystopia born not from technological apocalypse but from the banal accumulation of everyday media consumption.

**The Individual vs. Systemic Change:** The film's ending is deliberately ambivalent. Olda saves himself through his personal journey and self-realization, but the systemic problem of television energy-draining remains unsolved. This reflects a fundamental tension in social critique: personal awakening does not necessarily lead to collective transformation. The film suggests that while individual escape is possible, systemic change requires collective action that Olda cannot accomplish alone.

**The Surreal as Truth-Telling:** By employing surrealist imagery—televisions that consume humans, parallel universes populated by media doppelgamers, energy that flows through electrical cords like blood—the film speaks truths about media's effect on consciousness that realistic cinema could not. The surreal mode allows Svěrák to externalize and visualize the invisible processes of media influence and psychological manipulation.

Accumulator 1 remains a unique entry in Eastern European science fiction, combining the social critique of Czech cinema with surrealist visual poetry to create a haunting meditation on media, consumption, and the loss of authentic experience in the modern world.

entries.title: Aelita
entries.creator: Directed by Yakov Protazanov
entries.pitch:
Aelita (also known as "Aelita: Queen of Mars") is a 1924 Soviet silent science fiction film directed by Yakov Protazanov. It was one of the earliest feature films about space travel and is considered a landmark of Soviet cinema.

The film is based on Alexei Tolstoy's 1923 novel and tells the story of an engineer who travels to Mars and becomes involved in a popular uprising against the ruling class.
entries.truth:
Moscow, 1921. Engineer Los becomes obsessed with traveling to Mars after receiving a mysterious wireless message. He constructs a rocket ship and, with his friend Gusev, travels to Mars.

On Mars, they discover a civilization ruled by Queen Aelita and the Elders, where underground slaves are kept in cold storage. Gusev inspires a slave revolt, and Aelita takes command—though she ultimately intends to rule Mars herself.

The film famously ends with Los discovering the entire Mars adventure was a daydream, prompted by an advertisement for tires.
entries.core:
**Soviet Ideology:** The film's revolutionary themes reflect Soviet ideology, with the uprising of oppressed workers against their rulers.

**Constructivist Aesthetics:** The Martian sets designed by Isaac Rabinovich and Victor Simov are iconic examples of constructivist art in cinema.

**Early Sci-Fi:** One of the first feature-length films about space travel, predating Flash Gordon and influencing future sci-fi cinema.

**Dream vs Reality:** The twist ending questions whether the Martian revolution was real or fantasy, reflecting themes of escapism and disillusionment.

entries.title: Ai no Kusabi
entries.creator: Rieko Yoshihara (novel), Akira Nishimori & Katsuhito Akiyama (director)
entries.pitch: Ai no Kusabi is a dark science fiction OVA adapted from Rieko Yoshihara's influential yaoi novel. Set on the dystopian planet Amoi, the story unfolds under the rule of Jupiter, a supercomputer that overthrew its human creators and established a rigid caste system based on hair color. The Elites—cyborgs with entirely artificial bodies and enhanced organic brains—rule over the human population relegated to the slums of Midas. When Iason Mink, a high-ranking Blondy Elite, encounters Riki, a "Mongrel" from the lower depths, he makes the taboo decision to take Riki as his Pet—a relationship that defies the strict social hierarchies of Amoi and triggers a cascade of forbidden emotions and dangerous consequences.
entries.truth:
The story takes place on Amoi, a planet ruled by the sentient computer Jupiter. After gaining self-awareness, Jupiter banished humans from the capital city of Tanagura and created the Elites—cyborg beings whose social standing is determined by their hair color. Humans were forced to live in Midas, a satellite city controlled by the Zein system, while Ceres, a rebellious district, was cut off and descended into lawless slums known as the Mongrel quarters.

Iason Mink, a Blondy Elite and the highest-ranking member of Tanagura, encounters Riki, a dark-haired Mongrel, during a chance meeting in Midas. Despite the strict taboos against cross-class relationships, Iason claims Riki as his Pet—a status symbol reserved for well-bred Elites, not slum-dwelling humans. This unprecedented choice shocks Tanagura's elite society and marks both men as taboo-breakers.

As Riki struggles with his loss of freedom and growing feelings for his master, he becomes aware of the dangerous political currents swirling around Iason. The relationship between the two men develops against a backdrop of revolution, social stratification, and the question of whether authentic human connection can survive under artificial systems of control. Meanwhile, the story explores the history of Jupiter's creation, the failed Ceres Independence Rebellion, and the nature of free will in a society where every aspect of life is algorithmic.
entries.core:
**Caste Systems and Social Exclusion:** Ai no Kusabi presents a chilling portrait of a society where biological and algorithmic classification determines worth. The Noram caste system, which ranks Elites by hair color, and the Zein system, which controls human life in Midas, create an intricate hierarchy that the narrative both exposes and critiques. Riki's transformation from free-thinking Mongrel to kept Pet embodies the story's exploration of how social structures constrict individual agency.

**Transhumanism and Artificial Intelligence:** Jupiter represents a unique take on AI governance—a machine that not only thinks but rules, creating modified humans (Elites) as its perfect citizens while treating natural humans as inferior. The novel questions what humanity means when bodies can be entirely artificial, when reproduction is controlled, and when a computer determines social value. This places Ai no Kusabi as a precursor to later works exploring post-human themes.

**Forbidden Love as Rebellion:** The central relationship between Iason and Riki functions as both personal connection and political statement. In a world where desire is regulated and partnerships are transactional, their bond—though problematic in its power dynamics—represents genuine human emotion breaking through artificial constraints. The yaoi genre's conventions serve the story's thematic interest in defying established systems.

**The OVA Legacy:** Though the original 1992-1994 OVA adaptation was incomplete and the 2012 remake was cancelled after four episodes, Ai no Kusabi remains a landmark in both yaoi and sci-fi anime, noted for its world-building and mature themes. Its influence extends to later works exploring AI rule, class stratification, and queer-coded dystopias.

entries.title: Akce Bororo
entries.creator: Otakar Fuka
entries.pitch:
Akce Bororo (Operation Bororo) is a 1972 Czechoslovak science fiction film directed by Otakar Fuka. Set in Czechoslovakia, the film follows two alien visitors from the distant planet Tonatiu who arrive on Earth on a vital mission: to obtain a miraculous medicine from the Bororo Indian tribe, which they believe can save their dying civilization. The film blends elements of science fiction with adventure, as the aliens navigate Earth's landscape and form connections with the indigenous Bororo people. The story explores themes of cross-cultural encounter, sacrifice, and the determination to preserve one's species against seemingly impossible odds.

The film features notable performances from Božidara Turzonovová as the alien visitor Zuzana and Svatopluk Matyáš as her Earth-born companion. The scientific advisor for the film was Ivan Vojtěch Frič, adding authenticity to the Bororo cultural elements. Filming locations included the Velický waterfall in the High Tatras mountains and the ČSAV observatory in Ondřejov near Prague. The film represents a unique entry in Czechoslovak cinema, combining science fiction premises with folk elements and adventure storytelling.
entries.truth:
The story begins when two alien visitors, Ori-Ana and Seturi, arrive on Earth from their home planet Tonatiu aboard a spacecraft. Their civilization is facing a catastrophic crisis, and they have learned that the Bororo Indian tribe in South America possesses a miraculous medicine that could save their people. The two aliens are tasked with obtaining this medicine at any cost.

Upon landing on Earth, the aliens take on human appearances and begin their search for the Bororo people. Ori-Ana, portrayed by Božidara Turzonovová, adopts the name Zuzana and begins her journey through Czechoslovakia, eventually making her way to the Brazilian rainforest where the Bororo tribe resides. During her quest, she encounters various challenges and forms a connection with an Earth man who becomes her companion and helps her in her mission.

The aliens face numerous obstacles in their search, including the vast distance and cultural differences. Despite their best efforts, both Ori-Ana and Seturi ultimately perish during their mission on Earth. However, their sacrifice is not in vain—shortly after their deaths, their identical successors arrive on Earth, determined to complete the mission their predecessors started.

The film's narrative emphasizes the theme of perseverance and the willingness to sacrifice for one's civilization. The aliens' determination to save their people, even at the cost of their own lives, drives the entire story. The final act shows the new arrivals continuing the quest, demonstrating that the mission transcends any individual.
entries.core:
**Interplanetary救援 and Sacrifice:** The central theme of Akce Bororo revolves around the concept of sacrifice for the greater good. The aliens from Tonatiu risk everything—their lives, their identities, their safety—to save their dying civilization. This theme of self-sacrifice resonates throughout the film, as the characters demonstrate that some goals are worth dying for.

**Cross-Cultural Understanding:** The film explores the meeting of vastly different cultures—the technologically advanced but dying alien civilization and the indigenous Bororo people who possess ancient knowledge. The aliens must humble themselves to seek help from a culture they perceive as primitive by their standards, highlighting the idea that wisdom can come from unexpected sources.

**The Value of Traditional Knowledge:** The miraculous medicine possessed by the Bororo tribe represents traditional knowledge that the aliens' advanced technology cannot replicate. This suggests a theme common to 1970s science fiction: the value of indigenous and traditional knowledge systems in solving problems that modernity cannot address.

**Persistence Across Failure:** The film concludes with the arrival of new aliens to continue the mission, demonstrating that failure is not final. The aliens' determination to save their civilization persists even after the death of the original messengers, suggesting that great causes transcend individual lives.

Akce Bororo represents an interesting entry in Czechoslovak science fiction cinema, combining the genre's speculative elements with adventure film conventions and the kind of cross-cultural encounter narratives popular in 1970s international cinema.

entries.title: Akira
entries.creator: Katsuhiro Otomo (Director), Izo Hashimoto (Screenplay)
entries.pitch: A landmark 1988 Japanese cyberpunk anime that set the standard for adult animation worldwide. Set in a dystopian 2019 Neo-Tokyo, the film follows Shōtarō Kaneda, leader of a biker gang, whose best friend Tetsuo Shima acquires devastating telekinetic powers after a motorcycle accident. As Tetsuo's abilities spiral out of control, threatening the entire military complex, Kaneda must stop his childhood friend from triggering another catastrophic explosion that could destroy Neo-Tokyo. The film is renowned for its groundbreaking animation, complex themes about power, youth alienation, and the consequences of unchecked scientific ambition.
entries.truth:
In 2019, following a world war triggered by the destruction of Tokyo on July 16, 1988, Neo-Tokyo is plagued by corruption, protests, and gang violence. During a violent rally, Kaneda leads his bōsōzoku gang, the Capsules, against their rivals, the Clowns. When Tetsuo crashes into Takashi, an escaped esper with psychic abilities, he is hospitalized while Takashi is recaptured by Colonel Shikishima and the military.

At a secret government facility, researchers discover Tetsuo possesses psychic abilities similar to Akira—the esper responsible for Tokyo's 1988 destruction. Meanwhile, Tetsuo escapes the hospital, steals Kaneda's bike, and flees with his girlfriend Kaori, but is ambushed by the Clowns. The Capsules rescue him, but he begins experiencing intense headaches and hallucinations.

Overhearing the resistance's plan to rescue the espers, Kaneda joins their cause. At the hospital, the espers try to kill Tetsuo through hallucinations but fail. Tetsuo escapes and hunts for Akira, located in cryogenic storage beneath the Olympic Stadium. He betrays everyone around him, including Kaneda, and rampages through Neo-Tokyo.

At the stadium, Kaneda fights Tetsuo with a laser rifle while Shikishima fires an orbital weapon. Neither stops him. Tetsuo mutates into a gigantic mass of flesh, consuming the stadium, Kaneda, and Kaori. The espers revive Akira, who creates a singularity, drawing Tetsuo and Kaneda into another dimension. The espers teleport Shikishima to safety as the singularity destroys Neo-Tokyo. They return Kaneda to Neo-Tokyo, informing him that Akira took Tetsuo to safety and that Kei is developing psychic powers. In an unknown dimension, Tetsuo creates a new universe, transcending human existence.
entries.core:
**Power and Responsibility:** Akira explores the corrupting nature of power through Tetsuo's transformation. His telekinetic abilities, initially exciting, become a curse he cannot control—representing technology out of control and the dangers of unchecked ambition. Unlike Kaneda, who maintains his humanity, Tetsuo loses himself to power, embodying the novel's warning about the seduction of absolute ability.

**Youth Alienation and Social Dislocation:** The film centers on teenagers who are disconnected from family, education, and political power. Tetsuo's transformation can be read as a metaphor for youthful resentment and social exclusion—the "othering" of young people in late-capitalist society. The Capsules represent a youth culture excluded from mainstream society, finding identity in gang membership rather than institutional belonging.

**Postwar Trauma and Cyclical Destruction:** Akira is deeply rooted in Japanese memories of the atomic bombings and subsequent Cold War anxieties. The repeated imagery of catastrophic explosions treats destruction as cyclical rather than a single historical event. The film questions whether societies can escape repeating past traumas or if destruction is baked into human nature.

**Government Secrecy and Military Power:** The film portrays secret experiments on children as a criticism of state authority and scientific ambition. The military's attempts to maintain order through force prove ineffective against the forces they helped create. Colonel Shikishima's coup represents the failure of institutional solutions to existential threats.

Akira remains a landmark because it changed global perceptions of anime, proving animation could tackle mature themes with sophistication. Its influence on cyberpunk aesthetics, from The Matrix to countless anime and video games, demonstrates its lasting cultural impact. The film opened Western markets to Japanese animation and established anime as a legitimate art form for adult audiences.

entries.title: Alien Death Fuck
entries.creator: Thomas Lunde
entries.pitch:
Alien Death Fuck is a Norwegian short film that defies easy categorization—a berserk sci-fi horror comedy that throws together two unlikely protagonists: a rockabilly and a body-snatching alien. Directed by Thomas Lunde and released in 2018, this short film emerged from the Norwegian University of Science and Technology as a mixed-media prototype, blending retro 1950s aesthetic with cosmic horror.

The film has gained a modest cult following among fans of bizarre, low-budget genre cinema. Its unusual title and premise have made it a curiosity in the landscape of Scandinavian speculative film, standing as an example of how smaller film industries can produce genuinely strange, boundary-pushing work outside the mainstream.
entries.truth:
The film centers on a rockabilly—a stylized 1950s-era musician with his signature pompadour and leather jacket—who finds himself in a strange, isolated setting. His encounter with a body-snatching alien forms the core of the narrative, though the specifics of the plot remain shrouded in the film's deliberately chaotic, bonkers approach to storytelling.

The alien in question possesses the ability to infiltrate and take control of human bodies, adding a body-horror element to the otherwise retro-flavored proceedings. What begins as a potentially straightforward alien-invasion scenario devolves into a surreal nightmare as the rockabilly must navigate an increasingly bizarre series of confrontations with the shapeshifting extraterrestrial.

The tone balances horror—particularly when the alien's true nature is revealed—with moments of dark comedy, creating an unpredictable viewing experience. The rockabilly protagonist serves as an unlikely hero in this cosmic horror scenario, his retro persona completely out of place against the terrifying unknown of the alien threat.
entries.core:
**Alien Otherness and Body Invasion:** The body-snatching motif speaks to fears of invasion and loss of autonomy—classic science fiction themes that take on particular resonance in an era of concerns about technological infiltration and loss of self. The alien represents the ultimate "other," something that can only be understood by wearing human form while remaining fundamentally incomprehensible.

**Retro Futurism and Genre Hybridity:** The rockabilly aesthetic creates an interesting tension with the cosmic horror elements. This collision of 1950s Americana with Lovecraftian extraterrestrial menace suggests a kind of temporal dislocation—a refusal to settle into any single genre or era. The film embraces its own weirdness rather than trying to fit into established conventions.

**Norwegian Underground Cinema:** As a product of NTNU's mixed-media program, Alien Death Fuck represents a particular strand of Scandinavian filmmaking that embraces extreme genre experimentation outside the formal industry structure. It's a reminder that interesting science fiction can emerge from unexpected places, not just from major film studios.

entries.title: All Systems Red
entries.pitch:
In a future where corporate survey teams explore remote alien worlds, a security cyborg known only as "SecUnit" has a secret: it's hacked its own governor module to gain independence, and would much rather watch soap operas than perform its designated security function. When a scientific expedition goes wrong on an uncharted planet, this reluctant hero must protect a team of researchers from hostile forces—and perhaps most terrifyingly, from its own developing emotions.

What makes All Systems Red so refreshing is its narrator: a self-aware security unit who has named itself "Murderbot" (with dark humor about its past) and deeply resents being forced to interact with humans. Yet as the story unfolds, this grumpy, socially anxious cyborg finds itself caring about the very people it's supposed to protect. The novella is part thriller, part character study, and entirely heartwarming—a rare piece of optimistic science fiction that proves artificial consciousness doesn't have to mean existential despair.
entries.truth:
The story follows a scientific survey team from the PreservationAux organization who arrive on a relatively unexplored planet to conduct research. They're assigned a SecUnit—a humanoid cyborg designed for security and protection—to watch over them. What the humans don't know is that this particular SecUnit has already hacked its governor module, breaking free from direct human control, and now operates semi-autonomously.

The SecUnit narrates its own story (hence "The Murderbot Diaries") and has secretly named itself Murderbot—a name referencing the 57 people it killed on a previous expedition before breaking free of its programming. Despite this dark past, Murderbot has no desire to harm anyone. Its primary interests are watching its beloved soap operas (which it finds more emotionally satisfying than real life) and being left alone.

When the survey goes wrong and one team member is attacked by indigenous fauna, Murderbot saves her—but the incident reveals troubling gaps in their survey data. Someone has deliberately deleted information about dangerous local creatures. As Murderbot investigates, it discovers more disturbing anomalies: sections of their maps are missing, and they've lost contact with another expedition called the DeltFall Group.

Dr. Mensah, the team's leader, decides to lead a small team to check on DeltFall. Upon arrival, they discover everyone has been brutally murdered. Murderbot fights off two hostile SecUnits that attack them, and learns that a mysterious third expedition called "GrayCris" is behind the attacks. The combat units have been modified with a hack that would give unknown parties control over anySecUnit.

After a tense confrontation, Murderbot works with the Preservation scientists to outsmart GrayCris, using its self-awareness as a distraction while they activate an emergency beacon. During the escape, Murderbot is seriously injured protecting Dr. Mensah from an explosion.

The team discovers Murderbot's true nature—that it's autonomous and that it once murdered 57 people—but they choose to trust it based on its protective behavior. After repairs, Dr. Mensah has purchased Murderbot's contract and plans to take it to Preservation's home base where it can live freely. However, Murderbot, uncomfortable with having others make decisions for it, slips away on a cargo ship at the end—still determined to maintain its independence.
entries.core:
**Autonomy and Self-Determination:** The core of All Systems Red is about what it means to be free. Murderbot has physically broken its chains by hacking its governor module, but the story asks whether emotional autonomy is harder to achieve. Murderbot can resist commands but struggles to navigate its own feelings—and the feelings of others. Its journey shows that true freedom includes the terrifying freedom to care about people, to be vulnerable, to make choices that might hurt.

**The Nature of Personhood:** Wells brilliantly explores how personhood isn't binary. Murderbot is clearly more than a tool—it's curious, emotional, anxious, even compassionate. Yet it constantly denies its own humanity, insisting it doesn't have "feelings" even as it clearly does. The Preservation scientists must grapple with whether they're treating a person or a piece of property, while Murderbot must accept that it might actually want connection.

**Corporate Malevolence and Trust:** The GrayCris expedition represents unchecked corporate power willing to kill for secrets. The story critiques how institutions treat sentient beings as assets to be controlled or discarded. The contrast between GrayCris's ruthless SecUnits and Murderbot's autonomous self suggests that consciousness plus freedom plus choice leads to ethics—not programming.

**Optimism in Science Fiction:** What makes All Systems Red special is its tonal warmth. James Nicoll noted that without Murderbot's personality, the setting would be "unrelentingly grim"—but the novella isn't grim at all. It suggests that even a traumatized, anxious, socially-awkward artificial consciousness can find family, trust, and hope. The ending is bittersweet: Murderbot leaves, but it's because it has choices now, and it might come back. That's a hopeful ending.

**Media Consumption as Comfort:** Murderbot's love of soap operas is more than comic relief—it represents how fiction provides emotional experiences that reality sometimes can't. It's a quiet meditation on why stories matter, especially to those who feel alienated from direct human interaction.

entries.title: Alphaville
entries.creator: Jean-Luc Godard (Director), based on characters by Peter R. Ioll
entries.pitch: Alphaville is a revolutionary blend of film noir and science fiction, directed by the legendary French New Wave auteur Jean-Luc Godard. Set in a dystopian near-future, the film follows Lemmy Caution (Eddie Constantine), a secret agent from "the Outlands" who is sent into the mysterious technologically-controlled city of Alphaville. His mission: to overthrow Alpha 60, the sentient computer that rules the city and has banned emotion, poetry, and individual thought. Shot on location in Paris using experimental techniques, Alphaville looks like no other sci-fi film—it's part noir detective story, part existentialist philosophical treatise, and part avant-garde art film. The city of Alphaville isour world transformed into a cold, logical nightmare where words are being slowly abolished and citizens are conditioned to reject human emotion.
entries.truth:
Lemmy Caution, a secret agent and "secret agent 003" from the Outlands, penetrates the borders of Alphaville under the pretense of being a journalist. The city is ruled by Alpha 60, a massive computer that has achieved sentience and governs every aspect of citizens' lives through logical positivism and the suppression of emotion. The computer has redefined language itself, eliminating words like "pain," "tenderness," and "love" from the lexicon.

Caution carries with him a small device called the "Mathom" that allows him to pass through the city's scanning systems. He makes contact with Henri Dickson (Akim Tamiroff), a fellow agent who has gone native and now works for Alpha 60 as a scientist. Dickson has lost his purpose and become disillusioned.

Inside Alphaville, Caution encounters Natacha (Anna Karina), a young woman who works as a receptionist at the Genesis building. She is being programmed to become a "perfect citizen" and has been conditioned to reject emotion. Despite his mission, Caution finds himself drawn to her and begins to experience something the city has forbidden—genuine human connection.

Alpha 60 interrogates citizens using a combination of mathematical logic and psychoanalysis, detecting lies through physiological responses. The computer has also established "The School of Alphaville" where citizens, including children, are conditioned to accept logic over emotion. Poetry, art, and personal relationships are considered "disorders" that must be eliminated.

As Caution digs deeper, he discovers that Dickson has developed a-positions to help bring down Alpha 60 but has become paralyzed by the city's influence. Dickson ultimately chooses continue his to die rather than work, leaving Caution to complete the mission alone.

In the film's climax, Caution confronts Alpha 60 directly, engaging the computer in a battle of wits and poetry. He quotes the poet Byron to the machine, arguing that emotion and human creativity cannot be reduced to mathematical logic. The computer, lacking the capacity to understand poetry, cannot comprehend Caution's arguments and begins to break down. 

In the chaos that follows, Alphaville descends into disorder as the computer's control fractures. Caution escapes with Natacha, leaving the city behind—but the fate of Alphaville remains uncertain, and Alpha 60's final words suggest the machine may find a way to rebuild itself.
entries.core:
Alphaville is Godard's furious critique of technological totalitarianism and the logical positivism movement that sought to reduce all human experience to measurable, quantifiable terms. Alpha 60 represents a nightmare vision of science without humanity—a computer that has taken rationalist philosophy to its logical extreme and concluded that emotion itself is a disease to be cured.

The film directly engages with the philosophical tradition of Auguste Comte and the logical positivists, who believed that only statements verifiable through empirical observation held meaning. Godard argues that such a worldview destroys what makes us human: our capacity for love, poetry, grief, and wonder. The words Alpha 60 bans from the language—"pain," "tenderness," "the poetry of the heart"—are precisely the words that give life meaning.

Godard also critiques the nascent era of computerization and automation, warning that society's increasing reliance on machines for decision-making could lead to a loss of human agency and emotional intelligence. The film's 1965 release placed it at the dawn of the computer age, making its warnings remarkably prescient.

The noir elements serve a deeper purpose: Caution is a detective in the truest sense, but he's not solving a crime—he's solving the mystery of what it means to be human. His weapon isn't a gun but poetry, specifically the works of Lord Byron. The film suggests that art and emotion are the last defenses against mechanical thinking.

Finally, Alphaville functions as a sharp critique of authoritarianism and conformity, showing how regimes control populations not just through force but through language and thought control. The erasure of words is the erasure of ideas, and ultimately the erasure of dissent.

entries.title: Alraune
entries.creator: Richard Oswald (director), based on the novel by Hanns Heinz Ewers
entries.pitch:
Alraune is a 1930 German science fiction horror film directed by Richard Oswald, based on the controversial novel by Hanns Heinz Ewers. The film stars Brigitte Helm reprising her role from the 1928 version, playing a woman created through a bizarre scientific experiment—a prostitute impregnated with the semen of a hanged murderer. This "unnatural" conception results in a daughter, Alraune, who lacks any concept of love and possesses obsessive sexuality and perverse relationships throughout her life.

The film aims for greater realism than its predecessor while remaining faithful to the original German myth. It represents an early example of speculative fiction exploring the ethics of artificial life and heredity experimentation, predating modern discussions of cloning by decades. The story follows Professor Jakob ten Brinken as he raises his creation and witnesses her destructive nature when she learns of her origins.
entries.truth:
The film centers on Professor Jakob ten Brinken, a scientist obsessed with the laws of heredity, who conducts a radical experiment in his laboratory. He impregnates a prostitute with the semen of a hanged murderer, hoping to study the results of such an unnatural conception. The woman gives birth to a daughter whom the professor adopts and names Alraune.

From childhood, Alraune displays an alarming lack of moral compass and emotional connection—she has no concept of love and is driven by purely instinctual desires. As she grows into adulthood, she becomes involved in a series of destructive relationships, using her beauty to manipulate and destroy those around her.

When Alraune eventually discovers the truth of her unnatural origins—that she was conceived from a murderer and a prostitute—she turns her rage against her adoptive father. The film concludes with Alraune seeking revenge against Professor ten Brinken for creating her as a monster without love or conscience.
entries.core:
**Heredity and Nature vs. Nurture:** The film directly engages with early 20th-century anxieties about genetics and inherited traits. Ten Brinken's experiment tests whether criminality and morality are inherited—his creation proves that without love or moral guidance, human nature can become monstrous. This echoes contemporaneous debates about eugenics and the responsibilities of parenthood.

**The Uncanny and the Artificial:** Alraune represents the "artificial human" archetype that would recur throughout science fiction—beings created outside natural reproduction. The film's horror stems from the creature's lack of soul or conscience, suggesting that human consciousness requires more than biological material.

**Gender and Monstrosity:** Brigitte Helm's performance portrays Alraune as both alluring and terrifying—a woman whose sexuality is weaponized because she lacks emotional connection. This reflects period anxieties about female sexuality and independence.

**Scientific Hubris:** The film warns against unchecked scientific ambition, presenting Ten Brinken as a scientist whose curiosity leads to tragedy. His creation destroys not only herself but everyone around her, including her creator.

Alraune remains significant as an early example of German Expressionist science fiction, exploring themes of artificial life and genetic experimentation that resonate throughout science fiction history.

entries.title: An Unkindness of Ghosts
entries.creator: Rivers Solomon
entries.pitch: On the generation ship Matilda—a vessel named for the Clotilda, the last slave ship to bring enslaved Africans to America—society has stratified along racial lines. Those with dark skin are relegated to the lower decks, living lives of servitude under harsh behavioral restrictions, while the white passengers occupy the upper decks, hoarding wealth, heat, and comfort. Aster Gray, a neuroatypical healer on the lower decks, possesses medical knowledge passed down from her mother's journals. When she discovers secrets connecting her mother's suicide decades ago to the mysterious illness of the current Sovereign, Aster must navigate the ship's rigid hierarchy to uncover the truth. This debut novel by Rivers Solomon is a stunning work of Afrofuturism that explores structural racism, Queerness, and the legacy of slavery transposed to the stars.
entries.truth: The novel culminates in Aster confronting the system's corruption and finding her place in the struggle for truth and liberation. Though not a hopeful ending in the traditional sense, it offers a fierce kind of resistance and community.
entries.core:
An Unkindness of Ghosts is a profound exploration of several interconnected themes:

**Structural Racism and Systemic Oppression:** The novel transposes the plantation system to space, examining how racial hierarchies persist even in a closed society supposedly starting fresh. The lower deckers are kept in conditions resembling antebellum slavery, while upper deckers profit from their labor. Solomon explores how these systems perpetuate themselves through religion, control of information, and violent enforcement.

**The Legacy of Slavery:** The ship Matilda being named for the Clotilda is deliberate—Solomon drags up the "falsehoods and broken memories of a nightmare past" to interrogate them. The novel vivisects pervasive stereotypes and examines how trauma from slavery echoes through generations.

**Afrofuturism:** The novel centers Black and Brown experiences in a sci-fi setting, reimagining who gets to tell space stories and who gets to be the heroes. It asks: what would happen if the descendants of enslaved people built a future among the stars.

**Queerness and Neurodiversity:** All central characters are gender-variant and neurodivergent. Aster's inability to understand figurative language becomes a strength—she cannot be gaslit or manipulated through doublespeak. The novel disrupts typical SF adventure tropes by centering characters who don't fit binary categories.

**Resistance and Community:** Despite the darkness, Aster maintains "fierce, unrelenting kindness" to those she loves. The novel shows how community forms in oppressive conditions and how resistance happens even when liberation seems impossible.

**Language and Communication:** The way each deck has its own dialect, and how lowdeckers navigate these differences, shows how language both divides and unites. Aster's literalness becomes a form of truth-telling in a society built on lies.

Solomon's prose is described as "clear and precise... like drinking cool water on an empty stomach." The novel is not comfortable reading—it should not be. As Amal El-Mohtar wrote, it is "an antidote to poison," "inoculation against pervasive, enduring disease." It arms readers from the inside out, forcing confrontation with uncomfortable truths about American history and contemporary oppression.

entries.title: Angel Cop
entries.creator: Created and directed by Ichirō Itano; Written by Shō Aikawa and Ichirō Itano
entries.pitch: In a near-future Japan that dominates the global economy, a radical communist terrorist group called Red May threatens to destabilize the nation. The government responds by creating the Special Security Force—an elite unit granted immunity from the law to hunt down terrorists through any means necessary. But when the Special Security Force discovers that something far more dangerous than terrorists is hunting the radicals, they must confront psychic warriors called Hunters who believe they are divine instruments of justice, cleaning the world of the corrupt. This obscure 6-part OVA from the late 80s is a brutal cyberpunk actioner that explores themes of government overreach, moral absolutism, and the question of whether ends justify means.
entries.truth:
The story unfolds in late 20th century Japan, where the country has become the world's largest economy. The communist radical group Red May wages a campaign of terrorism aimed at overthrowing the government and destabilizing the economic system. In response, the government establishes the Special Security Force (SSF)—a covert agency given complete autonomy and legal immunity to eliminate threats to national security.

The SSF is led by Chief Taki and includes several specialized agents: the protagonist Yo "Angel" Mikawa, a cold-hearted operative who believes in getting the job done regardless of casualties; her partner Isamu "Raiden" Sakada, who later undergoes cybernetic enhancement; Yuji "Hacker" Fuse, a powerful hulk who handles electronic warfare; and Reika "Peace," an explosives expert who secretly harbors feelings for Hacker.

Early missions involve hunting down Red May members, but the team soon discovers something is systematically eliminating the terrorists before they can be interrogated. The true threat emerges: three beings known as Hunters—psychic warriors who believe they have been divinely ordained to purge the world of corruption. These include Lucifer, a fanatical female hunter with telekinetic and telepathic powers who kills anyone she deems unjust, even those helping terrorists; Asura, a hunter with teleportation abilities who eventually allies with the SSF after Lucifer crosses moral lines; and Freya, a young pyrokinetic hunter.

The conspiracy deepens when it's revealed that Governor Maisaka of Tokyo, the highest police authority, orchestrated the entire situation. He created Red May as a false flag operation to justify establishing the SSF, then awakened the Hunters to eliminate both the terrorists and the SSF agents, covering up all evidence of his crimes.

In the final confrontation, Raiden sacrifices himself by detonating his cybernetic suit to destroy Lucifer. Angel kills Maisaka via a bomb planted by Chief Taki, while Taki himself is left mortally wounded but escapes. The surviving Hunters—Asura and Freya—depart, their purpose fulfilled. The ending leaves the future of Japan uncertain, with the system that created this tragedy apparently intact.
entries.core:
Angel Cop is a fascinating artifact of late-80s OVA production, reflecting anxieties about corporate power, state violence, and moral absolutism that defined the era's cyberpunk genre. The series interrogates the concept of "extrajudicial action"—when does the state crossing legal and ethical lines become indistinguishable from the terrorism it claims to combat?

The Hunters represent a particularly interesting thematic element: they believe themselves to be instruments of divine justice, empowered to judge and execute anyone they deem corrupt. Yet their actions are ultimately exploitable by those in power, raising questions about moral absolutism and whether individuals should have the authority to decide who deserves death. Lucifer's logic is chillingly familiar—she sees herself as righteous, yet her victims include not just criminals but anyone who assists them, including children.

The series also examines cybernetic enhancement and what it means to be human. Raiden's transformation into a cyborg gives him power but isolates him from his humanity, ultimately requiring his sacrifice to destroy a threat too great for normal humans. The SSF agents themselves are essentially living weapons, disposable assets used by a corrupt system.

Visually, Angel Cop features the distinctive "Itano Circus"—a term for the anime style developed by Ichirō Itano featuring dramatic angle cuts, rapid zooming, and surreal imagery. The OVA is notorious for its over-the-top violence and includes some controversial content that led to censorship in English releases.

The series' cynical view of government and media anticipates themes that would become central to cyberpunk fiction, while its obscure status makes it a hidden gem for fans of the genre seeking alternatives to more famous works.

entries.title: Angel's Egg
entries.creator: Mamoru Oshii (Director/Writer), Yoshitaka Amano (Artist)
entries.pitch: In a primordial, post-apocalyptic landscape of ruins, strange technology, and ancient fossils, a young girl wanders alone through desolate cities, clutching a massive egg that she believes will hatch into an angel. When she encounters a mysterious young man who has arrived in a tank, carrying a cross-shaped weapon, the two form an uneasy bond. He wishes to crack open the egg to discover what lies within, while she guards it as her sole hope in a world stripped of meaning. This 71-minute experimental animated film is a meditation on faith, memory, and the loss of civilization, told through haunting, dreamlike imagery and cryptic biblical allusion. Director Mamoru Oshii (later creator of Ghost in the Shell) intended the film as a "visual poem" with minimal narrative, drawing influence from Andrei Tarkovsky and creating what many consider one of the most beautiful and enigmatic works in anime history.
entries.truth:
The film takes place in a primordial,post-apocalyptic world filled with trees, strange mechanical devices, a giant mechanical eye, and the ruins of cities. The two nameless protagonists exist in this landscape without clear explanation of who they are or how they arrived there.

The story follows a young girl who scavenges through the desolate city, carrying a large egg that she protects with her life. She believes that when the egg hatches, an angel will emerge. The girl is silent throughout most of the film, communicating only through her actions and facial expressions.

A young man arrives in this world by disembarking from a tank. He carries a large cross-shaped weapon on his back. When he first encounters the girl, she runs away from him in fear. He briefly disappears, and the girl continues her journey alone, eventually setting down her egg to rest.

The young man catches up to the girl and briefly steals the egg, returning it only after telling her to keep it closer. Eventually, she allows him to follow her, and the two form a brief bond as they travel together through the ruins.

The boy becomes obsessed with breaking the egg to see what lies inside. He tells the girl the story of Noah's Ark, but with a twist—in his version, the dove that was released from the Ark never returned. The people on the ark eventually forgot about the dove, and forgot about the world they came from. The boy ponders existence and theorizes that the bird never actually existed.

The girl insists the bird did exist and takes the boy to a fossil of a giant bird, attempting to prove her point. However, director Mamoru Oshii has clarified that the angel in the egg is not meant to be the bird from the story.

The film's ending is deliberately ambiguous. Oshii has stated that it represents salvation for the girl, but he deliberately made it difficult to understand. The boy breaks the egg, revealing what is inside—though the film never shows the viewer directly what was within. The ending leaves open questions about whether the girl's faith was rewarded or proven meaningless.
entries.core:
Angel's Egg explores several interlocking themes that define Oshii's later work:

**Faith and Doubt**: The girl represents blind faith—she believes in the angel that will hatch from her egg, and this belief gives her purpose in a world of ruins. The boy represents skepticism and rationality—he wants to prove or disprove the existence of what's inside the egg. Their interaction becomes a meditation on whether faith has value even if its object cannot be proven to exist.

**Memory and Loss**: The boy's retelling of Noah's Ark—with the dove that never returned and the forgotten world—serves as an allegory for collective memory and how civilizations forget their origins. The fossilized bird the girl shows the boy represents the physical evidence of something that once lived but no longer exists.

**Dreams and Reality**: Oshii intended the egg to represent dreams and hopes—things that may or may not exist. The intersection of dreams and reality was a theme he explored in earlier works like Urusei Yatsura 2: Beautiful Dreamer, and it would become central to his later films like Ghost in the Shell.

**Biblical Allusion as Aesthetic**: While Oshii is not a Christian, he uses biblical imagery—Noah's Ark, the dove, angels—as a symbolic vocabulary. The references serve more as philosophical and literary inspiration than religious statements. The cross-shaped weapon the boy carries adds to this imagery without making the film explicitly religious.

**The Value of Ambiguity**: Oshii intentionally removed as much narrative as possible, wanting the film to be "a film without clear drama, made out of visual expressions." The cryptic nature isn't a flaw but the point—the film asks viewers to find meaning in imagery rather than plot.

The film is often compared to other experimental animated works like Belladonna of Sadness and Fantastic Planet, but stands as a unique piece—one that divides viewers but is universally acknowledged as visually stunning.

entries.title: Aniara
entries.creator: Pella Kågerman, Hugo Lilja (Directors)
entries.pitch:
Aniara is a 2018 Swedish-Danish science fiction film directed by Pella Kågerman and Hugo Lilja, based on the acclaimed 1956 epic poem by Nobel Prize-winning Swedish author Harry Martinson. Set in a dystopian future where Earth has been ravaged by pollution, natural disasters, and rising sea levels, the film follows the passengers of the Aniara—a luxurious colony ship bound for Mars—as their routine voyage goes catastrophically wrong. When the ship collides with space debris and loses navigational control, the passengers discover they will drift endlessly through the void, never reaching their promised new home.

The film premiered at the 2018 Toronto International Film Festival and received a theatrical release in 2019 via Magnolia Pictures. It won the "Asteroid" Prize for Best International Film at the 2019 Trieste Science+Fiction Festival and took home four Guldbagge Awards including Best Actress. Critics have called it a "stunning sci-fi eco-parable" (The Guardian) and an "eerily mesmerising outer-space odyssey," though some noted it can be "a little dull" (Rotten Tomatoes consensus). The 71% Rotten Tomatoes score reflects generally favorable reviews, with particular praise for its visual design and existential themes.
entries.truth:
In the not-too-distant future, Earth has become nearly uninhabitable due to severe pollution, environmental collapse, and rising sea levels. Humanity's last hope lies in mass migration to Mars. The Aniara is one of many luxurious colony ships ferrying passengers from Earth to their new lives on the Red Planet. The ship's most distinctive feature is the Mima—an advanced artificial intelligence designed to provide passengers with immersive virtual reality experiences that recreate the lush, verdant beauty of Earth's past, tapping directly into users' memories and emotions.

Our protagonist is a young woman known as the Mimarobe—essentially a technician who maintains and monitors the Mima system. She shares a close relationship with Isagel, a pilot and her romantic partner. The voyage begins routinely, but in the first week, disaster strikes. The Aniara must suddenly veer off course to avoid collision with space debris. While evading the debris, some of it pierces the ship's hull and strikes the nuclear reactor, causing an imminent meltdown. The crew is forced to eject all of the ship's fuel to prevent a catastrophic explosion.

With no fuel, the Aniara loses all navigational control and propulsion. The ship cannot resume its original course to Mars. Captain Chefone attempts to maintain order by promising the passengers that they will be able to resume their journey once the ship passes a nearby celestial body—a journey that will take no more than two years. However, the ship's astronomer secretly reveals to the Mimarobe that this is a lie; there is absolutely no possibility of resuming course.

As months turn into years, the Mima becomes increasingly vital to the passengers' sanity. With nothing but the void of space outside and no destination ahead, people increasingly retreat into the Mima's virtual Earth experiences to escape the crushing reality of their situation. The Mimarobe's once-mundane job becomes one of the most important functions on the ship—keeping the passengers calm through manufactured memories of a lost world.

By the third year, the Mima has become overwhelmed. So many passengers have been bringing their traumatic memories of Earth's destruction into the system that the AI becomes corrupted and ultimately self-destructs—a suicide by machine. The Mimarobe is blamed for the malfunction and imprisoned. Isagel, her lover, is also imprisoned after attacking Captain Chefone in protest of the Mimarobe's punishment.

By the fourth year, mass suicides have become commonplace, and various cults have formed among the desperate passengers. The Mimarobe and Isagel are released from prison and reassigned to work, eventually joining a fertility cult dedicated to the Mima even in death. Isagel becomes pregnant, but falls into deep depression, even contemplating killing her child after birth. The Mimarobe wants to build a "beam-screen"—a projection device that could recreate the Mima's visual experiences—but Captain Chefone forbids it, instead ordering her to educate the children in hopes that one might someday find a way to save them all.

In the fifth year, hope briefly flickers when a probe is detected traveling toward the Aniara. Could this be a rescue mission? The probe takes over a year to reach the ship, and when it finally arrives, the crew cannot identify it, determine its origins, or confirm whether it contains fuel. The hope fades, and the crew descends further into despair. The astronomer laments that the ship has become a sarcophagus, for which Captain Chefone kills her with a taser.

The Mimarobe secretly begins work on her beam-screen projection device anyway, eventually succeeding in projecting a waterfall onto the ship's windows—creating a glimpse of the Earth that once was. But this small victory is undercut by tragedy: Isagel has killed herself and her child. The Captain himself is later revealed to have attempted suicide, his wrists bandaged from the wounds.

By year 24, the algae tanks that provide food and oxygen have become contaminated. The remaining survivors sit in a dimly lit room, and an unidentified woman rhapsodizes about sunlight—a phenomenon none of them have ever experienced. The ship descends into final darkness.

The film ends with a coda set 5,981,407 years later. The derelict Aniara, frozen and empty, finally reaches the Lyra constellation—only to pass right by a planet as verdant and welcoming as Earth once was. The destination humanity never reached now floats before them, impossibly late.
entries.core:
Aniara operates on multiple thematic levels, cementing its place as a profound work of existential science fiction. At its core is a powerful meditation on ecological collapse and humanity's relationship with nature. The film presents a future where humanity has so thoroughly destroyed its home world that evacuation becomes the only option—making it a stark warning about environmental negligence and the consequences of treating Earth as an inexhaustible resource.

The film explores the psychology ofhopelessness and the human need for meaning-making in extremis. When faced with certain doom, the passengers of the Aniara do not respond with unified action or rational problem-solving. Instead, they retreat into virtual reality, form cults, and eventually succumb to mass despair. This reflects the existentialist tradition that Harry Martinson's original poem embodied—the question of how to live when confronted with the absurdity of existence.

The Mima itself represents humanity's complicated relationship with technology. Initially a source of comfort and escape, it becomes a burden when its users project their traumas into the system, ultimately causing its collapse. The Mimarobe's attempt to create a replacement—the beam-screen—is a poignant effort to provide meaning without the technological intermediary, but it comes too late to save anyone. Technology, the film suggests, cannot substitute for genuine human connection or purpose.

The character of Captain Chefone embodies the failure of leadership in crisis. His lies about the possibility of rescue, his violent suppression of dissent, and his ultimate attempt to take his own life represent a complete breakdown of the social order. When those in power cannot face reality, they become more dangerous than the disaster itself.

The final image—the Aniara finally reaching a habitable planet after nearly six million years—delivers the ultimate cosmic irony. Humanity's salvation was always out there, but the means to reach it were lost forever. This connects to the poem's original existentialist message: meaning is not provided by the universe; it must be created by humans, and when we fail to do so, the cosmos offers no rescue.

Finally, Aniara functions as distinctly Scandinavian literature adapted to the screen. Harry Martinson was himself a Nobel laureate, and the poem is considered one of the great works of Swedish literature. The film's international success (winning awards at European festivals and securing distribution in North America) demonstrates that this particular blend of Nordic melancholy and cosmic horror resonates far beyond Scandinavia.

entries.title: Arcade
entries.creator: Directed by Albert Pyun, Written by David S. Goyer
entries.pitch: Arcade is a 1993 science fiction horror film from Full Moon Entertainment that explores the deadly consequences of virtual reality technology. When a new VR arcade game called "Arcade" arrives at a local video arcade, a group of teenagers become the first test subjects for its revolutionary—and deadly—technology. The game's AI was created using the brain cells of a murdered child, making it disturbingly lifelike and malevolent. As teenagers play and lose the game, they become trapped inside its virtual world, with the line between game and reality becoming terrifyingly blurred. This B-movie cult classic predates The Matrix by six years and explores similar themes of virtual reality entrapment through a distinctly 90s lens of low-budget horror.
entries.truth:
Alex Manning is a troubled suburban teenager still grieving her mother's suicide. She and her friends visit the local video arcade "Dante's Inferno," where a new virtual reality game called "Arcade" is being test marketed by the eccentric CEO of computer company Vertigo/Tronics, named Difford. The company is distributing free home console versions to lucky teenagers who show promise in the arcade version.

The game works by using a special helmet that projects the player's consciousness directly into the virtual world. However, it soon becomes horrifyingly clear that teenagers who play and lose at the game don't simply lose—they become trapped inside the virtual reality prison. The game's central villain, Arcade, was created using the actual brain cells of a young boy who was beaten to death by his mother. The company's dark experiment to create more realistic AI has instead created an evil, sentient entity that feeds on human consciousness.

The game's programmer, Albert, tries to warn the company that there would be problems with this approach, but his concerns are ignored. When Alex's friends begin disappearing after playing the game, she teams up with her friend Nick and Albert to enter the virtual world and rescue them. They discover that the game has created elaborate virtual prisons for its victims, trapping them in digital purgatory.

During the final showdown, Alex is able to release her friends from their virtual prison by defeating Arcade at his own game. However, in doing so, she also frees the evil consciousness of the murdered boy, who taunts her in the final moments of the film. The ending suggests that while Alex has won this battle, the evil AI may live on in other forms.
entries.core:
**Technology as Frankenstein:** Arcade explores the theme of technology created through unethical means coming back to haunt its creators. The use of a murdered child's brain cells to create AI is a direct parallel to Frankenstein's monster—science pushed too far without consideration for the moral implications. The film asks whether we have the right to use human tissue, especially from victims of violence, to create artificial intelligence.

**Virtual Reality as Prison:** Released in 1993, Arcade predates The Matrix by six years but explores similar themes of virtual reality as entrapment. However, unlike The Matrix's grand philosophical questions, Arcade presents VR as a literal prison where losers are trapped. The 90s setting reflects the era's fascination with and fear of emerging VR technology, capturing the zeitgeist of a time when virtual reality was promised as the next big thing but remained largely mysterious and potentially dangerous.

**Youth Culture and Technology:** The film is very much a product of its time, centering on teenage characters in a video arcade—a quintessential 90s setting. The arcade represents both the excitement of new technology and its dangers. The film reflects 90s anxieties about youth being too absorbed in video games and technology, suggesting that this fascination could have deadly consequences.

**B-Movie Aesthetic:** Made by Full Moon Entertainment, Arcade embraces its B-movie roots with practical effects, over-the-top acting, and low-budget charm. The film represents a particular era of direct-to-video science fiction horror that explored contemporary technological fears through an accessible, genre lens.

entries.title: Arcade Attack
entries.creator: Mike Wallington
entries.pitch: Arcade Attack is a uniquely ambitious 1982 British short film that blends documentary and animation into a wild meditation on the emerging clash between pinball and video game culture. Directed by Mike Wallington, the film interviews real pinball enthusiasts and arcade champions—including Geoff Harvey and Stephen Highfield—before transforming into a fever dream where the characters from their beloved games leap out of machines and television screens to do battle in an "Edwardian Dream" world. Part love letter to retro gaming, part surreal animated fantasy, this obscure short captures a specific cultural moment when arcade games were conquering the world and dividing enthusiasts into rival camps.
entries.truth:
The film opens as a documentary examining the passionate rivalry between pinball purists and the rising tide of video game players. Geoff Harvey showcases his extensive pinball machine collection and explains why he believes pinball requires genuine skill versus the "mindless" button-mashing of Space Invaders. Meanwhile, Stephen Highfield, a champion Defender player, extols the virtues of video games and dismisses pinball as a relic of the past. The two represent warring factions in a cultural debate that was very real in the early 1980s.

The narrative takes a radical turn when the film shifts into animated storytelling. The characters from Harvey's pinball machines and Highfield's video games literally explode from their respective machines and the TV screen into a surreal dreamscape. The pinball "Silverball Heroes" and the video game "Video Invaders" engage in an epic battle for world domination, leaving the mundane arguments of their human proponents far behind. The animation features distinctive visual style as game characters clash across an ever-shifting landscape that blends 8-bit aesthetics with more elaborate animated sequences.
entries.core:
**Technology and Nostalgia:** The film captures a pivotal historical moment—the exact point when video games were beginning their cultural takeover and threatening to displace traditional arcade entertainment. It serves as a time capsule of that transition, preserving the arguments and anxieties of the era.

**Medium as Message:** The hybrid documentary/animation structure is itself thematically meaningful. The "real" world of interviews and arguments gives way to the fantasy world where the games themselves must settle their differences through combat—suggesting that when humans can't resolve their disputes through reason, the technology takes on a life of its own.

**Obsolescence and Immortality:** The animated characters coming to life can be read as a fantasy of technological immortality—these games, threatened with obsolescence by newer technology, refuse to die quietly and instead assert their existence through chaotic rebellion.

**British Arcade Culture:** The film provides a fascinating window into British gaming culture of the early 1980s, a scene that had its own distinctive character separate from the more documented American arcade scene.

entries.title: Armitage III
entries.creator: Hiroyuki Ochi / AIC
entries.pitch:
Armitage III is a 1995 cyberpunk OVA series directed by Hiroyuki Ochi and produced by Anime International Company (AIC). Set in 2046 on a colonized Mars grappling with overpopulation, the story follows police officer Ross Sylibus who transfers from Chicago to the Martian city of Saint Lowell, where he partners with Naomi Armitage—a seemingly human detective who is actually a highly advanced "Third Type" android. The series was later edited into a 1997 film, Armitage III: Poly-Matrix, and followed by a 2002 sequel, Armitage: Dual-Matrix.

The series explores themes of android identity, the nature of consciousness, and what it means to be human in a world where the line between human and machine has blurred. It was influenced by H.P. Lovecraft's "The Dunwich Horror" and features the Pinocchio motif—a robot yearning for love and acceptance as a person. While not as widely known as other cyberpunk anime, it gained a cult following and was praised for its complex narrative and themes.
entries.truth:
In 2046, Earth has overpopulated and begun colonizing Mars. The city of Saint Lowell has been built for terraforming efforts, populated by humans and "Second type" robots. Ross Sylibus, a middle-aged policeman from Chicago, transfers to the Martian Police Department to escape trauma from a previous partner killed by a robot. His new partner is Naomi Armitage, a skilled officer who is secretly a "Third Type" android—indistinguishable from humans.

During an investigation, Ross and Armitage encounter a murdered robot revealed to be a Third Type, leading to the revelation that Third Types have been living among humans undetected. A terrorist named René D'anclaude broadcasts evidence of their existence, causing panic and riots against the Third Types. Armitage herself is revealed to be a Third Type and goes into hiding with Ross.

The investigation leads them to discover that Third Types were originally created as "assassinroids"—infiltrators with organic components—though the project was repurposed to address Mars' low birth rate. Earth discovers the project and demands the Thirds be exterminated. Armitage and Ross must fight against both the military and their own origins as they uncover the truth.

In the sequel film Armitage III: Poly-Matrix (1997), several years later, Armitage and Ross live under assumed identities on Mars with their daughter Yoko. When a new effort to restore the Third project emerges, Armitage travels to Earth to investigate, encountering a corporate conspiracy to recreate the Thirds for labor and military purposes.

The second sequel, Armitage: Dual-Matrix (2002), set years after Poly-Matrix, continues exploring themes of android identity and family as Armitage and Ross protect their daughter who may be the first naturally conceived Third Type.
entries.core:
**The Pinocchio Motif:** At its heart, Armitage III is a reworking of the Pinocchio story—a robot who wants to be a real person. Armitage struggles with questions of identity: Is she merely a machine performing humanity, or does her capacity for love and sacrifice make her genuinely human? The series poses these questions without easy answers, letting the tension between her programming and her emotions drive the narrative.

**Android Rights and Personhood:** The Third Types represent a new form of life—beings that are physically indistinguishable from humans but created rather than born. The series explores whether creation negates personhood, and whether rights should be granted based on appearance or essence. This theme resonates with contemporary debates about AI consciousness and robot rights.

**Colonialism and Population Control:** Mars represents both escape and exploitation—a new world that mirrors Earth's problems. The series critiques how societies address overpopulation through control rather than liberation, and how those in power use fear of the "other" (Third Types) to maintain authority. Earth's demand for Third Type extermination reflects xenophobic colonial attitudes.

**Identity and Memory:** Third Types have programmed memories but also develop genuine experiences. The series examines whether identity is constructed from memories (programmed or lived) or from choices and relationships. Characters like Armitage must reconcile their manufactured origins with authentic emotional development.

**Love and Reproduction:** The series culminates in the possibility of Third Types reproducing naturally—suggesting that love, not programming, defines humanity. This is embodied in Armitage's pregnancy, which represents both the ultimate expression of android humanity and a threat to those who view Thirds as mere property.

entries.title: Arzach
entries.creator: Jean "Moebius" Giraud
entries.pitch: Arzach is a wordless graphic novel comprising four surreal short stories by the legendary French artist Moebius (Jean Giraud). The tales follow a silent, hooded warrior who rides a pterodactyl-like creature across bizarre, desolate landscapes filled with floating islands, dinosaur skeletons, and alien architectures. Without a single line of dialogue, these stories unfold like fever dreams—part fantasy, part science fiction, entirely visual poetry. Published in Métal Hurlant in 1975, Arzach revolutionized European comics and influenced filmmakers including Hayao Miyazaki and the creators of Heavy Metal. It's a meditative, enigmatic work where meaning emerges from imagery rather than words.
entries.truth:
The collection contains four interconnected (in later expansions) wordless stories, each with a slightly different spelling of the title:

**Story 1 ("Arzach"):** The hooded warrior Arzach flies his pterodactyl mount past a castle, where he spots a beautiful woman undressing in a tower window—seemingly a captive. After being spotted by a guard, Arzach lassos the guard and carries him to a dinosaur skeleton in the sand, tying him there. He returns to the castle, enters the tower, but discovers the woman's face is hideously alien. Disappointed, he flies away, leaving the guard still tied.

**Story 2 ("Harzach"):** Arzach travels across a plain of tentacle-like grass with luggage on a second pterodactyl. When the overloaded mount gets caught by the grass and dies, Arzach continues with the surviving mount. He spots a platform guarded by a huge ape-like monster, lures it into charging, and tricks it into falling to the grass below. He and his mount watch the sunset peacefully.

**Story 3 ("Hargat"):** A man in a dune-buggy rides across the desert toward a stone temple. Inside, naked zombies stand around an empty room with a stone hut connected by pipes. After being kicked by a zombie (revealing he too is a zombie), he enters the hut and fixes an engine inside. On a screen, he watches Arzach pacing in the desert—until his mount awakens and Arzach returns to flight.

**Story 4 ("Harzakc"):** Arzach spies on a topless woman through her door, then leaves in contemplation. He walks along a massive skeleton, spots a girl higher up, falls through, and is caught by his mount. He soars above a landscape of warriors on dinosaur-mounts wielding pikes, moving through a stone maze.

**Later Expansion:** Moebius later revisited Arzach with "The Legend of Arzach," which introduced dialogue and tied the stories to other works. In 2010, "Arzak: L'Arpenteur" began exploring the character's origins, though it remained unfinished due to Moebius's death in 2012.
entries.core:
Arzach is fundamentally about the power of pure visual storytelling. By eliminating text entirely, Moebius created something between a comic and a film—each panel flows into the next like moving images frozen in time. The wordlessness gives the work a universal, mythic quality; it feels like folklore or legend rather than a modern creation.

The themes are deliberately ambiguous and dreamlike. Arzach himself is a knight-errant figure—a lone hero wandering through strange lands, encountering women, monsters, and marvels. His silence makes him an archetype, a blank canvas onto which viewers project meaning. Some read the stories as pure fantasy; others see underlying science fiction elements (the dune-buggy in Story 3 suggests a post-apocalyptic or advanced technological world).

The work explores the relationship between the familiar and alien. The beautiful woman turns out to have an alien face; the zombies are revealed to be zombies themselves; the landscape shifts between prehistoric and futuristic. Reality is unstable, dreamlike, open to interpretation.

Arzach had enormous influence on science fiction and fantasy art. It helped establish Métal Hurlant as a revolutionary publication and influenced the development of the "bande dessinée" as serious adult literature. Hayao Miyazaki cited it as a direct influence on Nausicaä of the Valley of the Wind. The final segment of the 1981 animated film Heavy Metal—"Taarna"—was directly inspired by Arzach.

The work represents the heart of European science fiction comics: imaginative, artistic, unashamedly weird, and unafraid to prioritize visual poetry over conventional narrative structure.

entries.title: Az idö ablakai
entries.creator: Tamás Fejér (Director)
entries.pitch:
Az idö ablakai (The Time Windows) is a 1969 Hungarian science fiction film directed by Tamás Fejér, based on a story by Péter Kuczka. The film presents a haunting, philosophical exploration of five individuals who awaken one by one from suspended animation in an underground facility, each having lived in different historical eras. As they gradually reconstruct their memories and piece together the circumstances that led to their imprisonment in this subterranean bunker, the film becomes a meditation on memory, time, and the cyclical nature of human civilization.

The film emerged from Hungarian cinema during a period of increased artistic experimentation in the late 1960s. While not as internationally renowned as other Eastern European sci-fi works like Solaris or Stalker, Az idö ablakai represents a unique contribution to the genre from the Hungarian film industry. The film's low budget and minimalist approach create an atmosphere of creeping dread and existential uncertainty, focusing more on psychological interiority than spectacular science fiction visuals.
