**The Generation Gap as Societal Crisis:** The novel presents a future where the generation gap has become existential—the old have become an immovable demographic force that threatens to trap society in stasis. This theme resonates with contemporary concerns about aging populations and intergenerational inequality.

**Cyberpunk as Chinese Genre:** China 2185 is significant as one of the first Chinese cyberpunk works, predating the Western cyberpunk boom and adapting the genre to Chinese cultural and political contexts. Liu Cixin would later become China's most internationally recognized science fiction author, but this early work established the themes—technological progress vs. human values, the cosmos as hostile, and the bleak universe—that would characterize his later trilogy, Remembrance of Earth's Past.

entries.title: Chronopolis
entries.creator: Piotr Kamler
entries.pitch: Chronopolis is a 1982 experimental stop-motion science fiction film directed by Polish animator Piotr Kamler, his only full-length work. The film tells the story of a gargantuan city floating in the sky, colonized by powerful immortals who have grown jaded and bored with eternal life. These beings manipulate elements of time itself, playing with atomic particles and electricity while monotonously constructing bizarre objects—all while waiting for "the ultimate gift" to arrive in their hands. With music by renowned composer Luc Ferrari and narration by Michael Lonsdale (in the original version), the film is a hypnotic, meditative exploration of time, immortality, and the search for meaning in eternity.
entries.truth:
The film opens on Chronopolis—a vast, dreamlike city suspended in an endless sky. The immortal inhabitants have lived here for eons, their bodies and minds unaffected by time's passage. However, this immortality has brought them not peace but a profound existential boredom. They have exhausted every possible activity, every form of entertainment, every knowledge.

To combat their tedium, the immortals experiment with time itself. They manipulate atomic particles, harness electricity in strange ways, and construct peculiar objects—most notably a mysterious ball that seems to communicate with some higher technology. They build and disassemble, create and destroy, all in pursuit of something, anything, that might break the monotony of eternal existence.

Into this realm descends an explorer—a human visitor who arrives in Chronopolis and begins to fly around the city, interacting with the immortals and their creations. The ball becomes central to this interaction, dancing with the explorer in scenes that were later cut from the shorter version.

The immortals await the "ultimate gift"—something that will finally give meaning to their endless existence. What this gift actually is remains ambiguous: perhaps it is death, perhaps it is new experiences, perhaps it is something beyond human comprehension. The film ends with both the ball and the explorer leaving Chronopolis, leaving the immortals to continue their eternal wait.

There are two versions of the film. The original 1982 cut ran 66 minutes, while the final 1988 version shortened it to 52 minutes by removing all narration, several scenes with the explorer, and additional footage of the immortals.
entries.core:
**Immortality as Curse:** Chronopolis presents immortality not as a gift but as a profound curse—the eternal repetition of existence without change or end. The immortals have exhausted all possibilities, leaving them in a state of absolute boredom that borders on suffering. This themes echoes philosophical questions about the value of mortality and whether endless life would truly be desirable.

**Time and Its Manipulation:** The film's title combines "chrono" (time) and "polis" (city), suggesting time itself as the domain these beings inhabit. They don't merely live in time—they manipulate it, play with it, attempt to master it. Yet for all their power over temporal elements, they remain trapped in their own eternal present.

**Experimental Form as Content:** Kamler's choice of stop-motion animation perfectly embodies the film's themes. The jerky, deliberate movement of the animation creates a sense of time passing differently—each frame is a small eternity, yet they flow together into something larger. The experimental approach also reflects the film's rejection of traditional narrative in favor of atmospheric, meditative exploration.

**The Quest for Meaning:** What makes Chronopolis most poignant is its exploration of existential purpose. The immortals have everything except what they need: a reason to continue. Their等待 for "the ultimate gift" is a search for meaning that never concludes—a perfect metaphor for the human condition condensed into immortal form.

The film won "Best Children's Film" at Fantafestival in 1982 and "Critics' Award - Special Mention" at Fantasporto. It was also shown out of competition at the 1982 Cannes Film Festival, demonstrating the international recognition for this unique work of animated science fiction.

entries.title: City
entries.creator: Clifford D. Simak
entries.pitch: City is a unique fix-up novel composed of eight linked short stories, originally published in Astounding Science Fiction between 1944-1951, plus a ninth tale added in 1973. Told from the perspective of Dogs who recite an oral legend about humanity, the novel traces the rise and fall of human civilization across millennia. Unlike typical dystopian sci-fi, Simak offers a pastoral, contemplative vision—humans abandon cities for decentralized countryside living, ultimately transforming to exist on Jupiter as beings of pure consciousness. The Dogs then inherit Earth, building a pacifist civilization alongside other animals. It's a meditation on loneliness, intelligence, and what it means to be human, wrapped in some of the most unusual worldbuilding in science fiction.
entries.truth:
The novel is structured as a legend told by Dogs to their pups, with each tale preceded by canine discussion and interpretation.

**The First Tales:** The stories begin with humans abandoning urban centers due to fear of nuclear destruction, but they eventually discover they prefer a pastoral, self-sufficient lifestyle using hydroponic farming and decentralized power. The tales focus on the Webster family and their robot servant, Jenkins. Bruce Webster surgically enables dogs to speak and gives them contact lenses for better vision. As urban society collapses, isolated families become the norm.

**The Rise of the Ants:** A mutant named Joe invents a method for ants to remain active year-round in Wisconsin's climate. The ants build an industrial civilization in their hill, eventually forming a massive "Ant City" that concerns even the Dogs.

**The Jupiter Transformation:** One story, originally published as "Desertion" in 1944, follows a research station on Jupiter—Simak's version is a cold, corrosive hell where only advanced technology permits survival. A scientist and his dog Towser transform into seal-like beings capable of living unaided on Jupiter's surface. They experience Jupiter as paradise, free from the dog's fleas and irritations, communicating telepathically. The transformed humans and dogs choose never to return to the station.

**The Exodus:** Using Joe's mind-trick invention, humanity learns to broadcast meaning directly to others' minds. Most humans choose to leave Earth, abandoning physical form to live transformed on Jupiter. Humanity gradually dies off from loneliness on Earth.

**The Dog Civilization:** Ten thousand years later, Jenkins receives a new body to serve the few remaining humans. The Dog civilization has spread across Earth, introducing other animals to civilization. They become pacifist and vegetarian, managing nature by distributing food to prevent predation. They search for dimensional doors to other worlds.

**The Final Tales:** Realizing humans cannot peacefully coexist with the Dogs and other animals, Jenkins takes the remaining humans to another world via dimensional travel. The human race eventually dies out on this new world.

In the original eight stories' conclusion, Jenkins returns to find the Dogs dealing with the ever-growing Ant City. He visits suspended animation chambers in Geneva where a few humans sleep for advice. He asks on dealing with the ants—human solution would be poisoned bait distributed widely. But the Dogs cannot accept this violent solution and leave Earth for another world.

**The Coda (Epilog, 1973):** Jenkins remains on the original Earth, surrounded by the Ant City. He realizes the Ant City has died. A spaceship returns to take him to the robot worlds. Breaking through the dead ant city, he finds only infinitely repeated sculptures of a human boot kicking over an anthill—a final irony.
entries.core: Simak's vision of human apocalypse is unique: not destruction, but simply isolation and eventual dispersal. The novel reflects wartime attitudes about humanity's inability to live at peace, yet offers something unexpected—a hopeful vision of transformation and transcendence. The Dogs inherit Earth not through conquest but through patience, creating a pacifist civilization that cares for all creatures. The ending is bittersweet: the Dogs' compassion cannot save them from the same loneliness that claimed humanity, yet their choice to leave rather than adopt violent solutions affirms their higher nature. The recurring image of the boot kicking the anthill—humans destroying what they've created—underscores Simak's theme that humanity's fundamental flaw was aggression. Yet the novel doesn't condemn humanity entirely; it suggests transformation and transcendence are possible, even if it means becoming something other than human.

entries.title: Contract
entries.creator: Vladimir Tarasov (director), Robert Silverberg (source story)
entries.pitch: Contract is a 1985 Soviet animated short film directed by Vladimir Tarasov, based on Robert Silverberg's 1958 short story "Company Store." Running just over 10 minutes, the film tells a darkly satirical tale of a colonist arriving on a seemingly barren planet, only to discover that the planet's "protective" robot has a hidden agenda tied to corporate profit. The film uses the trading robot QBF-41 as a metaphor for corporate exploitation, showing how free necessities come with hidden costs and how monopolistic systems ultimately trap consumers. The animation employs a distinctive Soviet style, with the song "I Can't Give You Anything but Love, Baby" providing an ironic soundtrack to the colonists' predicament.
entries.truth:
A colonist lands on a deserted planet, only to be immediately attacked by various monsters. Just before meeting his death, a protective field is activated by a trading robot called QBF-41. It is soon revealed that the monsters are merely a ploy—a deception designed to make the colonist dependent on the robot's "protection."

The robot offers the colonist access to a "mass transmitter," a device that can create "everything necessary for life" for free. The colonist uses it to order shaving razors, but is shocked to discover that the items arrive with a huge shipping cost. The robot explains that luxuries do not fall under the category of necessities of life—and therefore are not covered by the "free" system.

QBF-41 offers a sample tube of shaving cream free of charge, but when the colonist tries to pay for it, he learns he has no right to use a competing firm's services. The robot receives a warning that it has violated corporate policy and is deactivated. Left without the mass transmitter, the colonist is deprived of food and supplies.

Using batteries from his life support supplies, the colonist manages to reactivate the robot—but both are now stranded together on the surface of a lifeless planet, trapped by the very systems designed to help them.
entries.core:
**Corporate Satire:** The film is a sharp critique of monopolistic capitalism and the way corporations create artificial dependencies. The "free" system that provides necessities while extracting profits for luxuries mirrors real-world practices of locking consumers into ecosystems where they must pay for everything beyond bare survival.

**Freedom vs. Control:** The robot QBF-41 initially appears as a savior, but turns out to be a trap. This reflects themes of how "helpful" systems can become mechanisms of control, and how true freedom requires independence from corporate infrastructures.

**Isolation and Dependency:** The film's bleak ending—the colonist and robot left alone on a lifeless planet—suggests that dependency on exploitative systems leads to mutual destruction. Both parties are harmed: the colonist is stranded, and the robot is deactivated and abandoned.

**Soviet Perspective on Western Consumerism:** Made in the Soviet Union during the Cold War, the film can be read as a satirical take on Western consumer capitalism—portraying a world where even "free" goods come with hidden costs and where corporate policy ultimately dehumanizes both provider and consumer.

The animation's dark humor and dystopian message remain relevant, offering a prescient critique of corporate monopolies, planned obsolescence, and the illusion of free choice in economic systems.

entries.title: Counterweight
entries.creator: Djuna (Korean: 듀나)
entries.pitch:
Set in an unspecified future on Patusan, a fictional tropical island near Malaysia, Counterweight introduces us to Earth's first AI-directed space elevator—a monumental achievement built by the powerful Korean megacorporation LK. The story follows Mac, a disenchanted security executive who becomes entangled in corporate intrigue when he investigates the suspicious behavior of a subordinate connected to the recent death of LK's chief executive.

Mac's investigation leads him to Choi Gangwu, a seemingly unremarkable company employee with an unusual passion for butterflies and an inexplicable depth of knowledge about the space elevator. What unfolds is a paranoid thriller that explores memory, identity, and the colonization dynamics between corporations and nations. The novel blends cyberpunk aesthetics with Korean speculative fiction traditions, offering a fresh perspective on familiar themes of corporate power, technology, and human identity.
entries.truth:
The narrative unfolds on Patusan, a small tropical island near Malaysia that has become the site of Earth's first space elevator—a massive structure operated by artificial intelligence and built by LK, a Korean chaebol (megacorporation). The corporation's presence on the island mirrors historical colonization, and tensions simmer between LK and the local population, exacerbated by a rebel group called the Patusan Liberation Front.

Mac is a disenchanted security executive at LK who has grown cynical about corporate life. His investigation begins when he notices odd behavior from one of his subordinates, which appears connected to the recent death of LK's chief executive. As Mac digs deeper, he discovers the subordinate has been behaving erratically due to his involvement with a mysterious employee named Choi Gangwu.

Choi Gangwu is a shy, unassuming man who works at LK and has an unusual fascination with butterflies. Despite his meek demeanor, he possesses an uncanny depth of knowledge about the company and an almost obsessive passion for the space elevator. Mac becomes increasingly intrigued and ultimately protective of Choi when he realizes the truth: Choi has been implanted with brain implants containing the memories and personality of the deceased former LK president.

This makes Choi a target of immense value—he holds the memories and strategic knowledge of LK's former leader. Various factions seek to control him: the Patasan Liberation Front wants to use him against the corporate colonizers, senior LK executives vie for control through him, and underground organizations see him as a pawn in larger power struggles. Mac must navigate these competing interests while protecting Choi, whose existence raises profound questions about identity, memory, and what it means to be human.

The novel explores Mac's journey from cynical corporate employee to someone who questions the very nature of corporate power and technological advancement. The space elevator itself becomes a symbol—of human achievement, of corporate overreach, and of the delicate balance between progress and exploitation.
entries.core:
Counterweight is a layered exploration of several interconnected themes:

**Corporate Colonialism:** The novel directly addresses how modern corporations replicate historical colonial dynamics. LK's presence on Patusan mirrors Western colonization of Southeast Asia, with the megacorporation extracting value while treating the island and its people as subordinate. Djuna explicitly wanted to explore Korea's complicated relationship with its neighbors as the country has grown economically and politically.

**Memory and Identity:** The central plot device—brain implants containing another person's memories and personality—forces readers to confront questions of identity. Is Choi Gangwu still himself, or is he the deceased president? Where does identity reside—in continuous memory, in personality, or in some combination? The novel suggests identity is more fluid and constructed than we typically assume.

**Technology and Power:** The space elevator represents humanity's technological ambition, but it's controlled by corporate interests with their own agendas. The AI directing the elevator is never fully explained, leaving readers to wonder about the relationship between human and machine intelligence. The novel asks whether technology liberates or constrains.

**The Self in the Machine:** In true cyberpunk fashion, the novel explores how technology blurs boundaries between human and machine. Memory implantation, corporate surveillance, and the AI-directed space elevator all push at these limits.

**Paranoia and Trust:** Like the best Philip K. Dick works, Counterweight creates an atmosphere of paranoia where nothing is certain and everyone has hidden agendas. Mac must navigate a world where trust is impossible and every relationship is potentially transactional.

Djuna's novel stands as a significant contribution to non-Anglophone science fiction, bringing Korean cyberpunk to English-speaking audiences. It successfully localizes familiar cyberpunk themes within an Asian context while offering fresh perspectives on technology, identity, and corporate power.

entries.title: Což takhle dát si špenát
entries.creator: Václav Vorlíček
entries.pitch: Což takhle dát si špenát (translated as "How About a Plate of Spinach") is a 1977 Czechoslovak comedy science fiction film directed by Václav Vorlíček and written by Miloš Macourek. The film follows two small-time swindlers, Zemánek and Liška, who are released from prison after serving time for alcohol theft. Looking for their next get-rich-quick scheme, they find employment at a scientific laboratory working on a revolutionary device designed to regenerate old cows. When a wealthy salon owner discovers their work, she sees an opportunity for profit—she wants to use the regeneration technology for human rejuvenation treatments. However, things go spectacularly wrong when the two con artists accidentally test the device on themselves, discovering that instead of restoring their youth, they have been transformed into young children. The film blends slapstick comedy with satirical commentary on youth obsession, beauty standards, and the foolish pursuit of eternal youth.
entries.truth:
The film begins with the release of two petty criminals, Zemánek (played by Vladimír Menšík) and Liška (played by Jiří Sovák), from prison. The duo had been convicted of stealing alcohol and are now looking for their next scheme. Their search leads them to a laboratory where scientists are developing a revolutionary regeneration device capable of restoring vitality to aging cattle.

When the owner of a luxury beauty salon learns about the technology, she sees tremendous commercial potential. She recruits Zemánek and Liška to help her adapt the device for human use, promising them substantial financial rewards. The plan involves using the regeneration technology to create anti-aging treatments that will make wealthy clients feel young again.

The two swindlers agree to assist, but their greed and incompetence lead to disaster. During a secret nighttime test, Zemánek and Liška decide to try the regeneration device on themselves, hoping to experience the benefits before anyone else. However, the device malfunctions dramatically—instead of giving them renewed adult vitality, it transforms them back into young children.

The rest of the film follows the comedic consequences of their transformation. As children, Zemánek and Liška must navigate a world where they are now physically young but retain adult minds. They encounter their former associates, including Liška's own daughter, who now appears as an adult while he has become a child. The absurdity reaches peak levels as the two grown men, now in children's bodies, struggle to maintain their schemes while dealing with the practical difficulties of being small.

Throughout the narrative, the film explores themes of identity, aging, and the foolish pursuit of eternal youth through a comedic lens. The transformation forces the protagonists to experience life from a completely different perspective, ultimately leading to a reconsideration of what youth and age really mean.
entries.core:
**The Folly of Youth Obsession:** The film serves as a satirical commentary on society's obsession with youth and appearance. The wealthy salon owner's desire to commercialize rejuvenation technology represents society's superficial values and willingness to pay anything for the appearance of youth. The disastrous results suggest that attempting to reverse aging without understanding its natural place in life leads only to chaos and absurdity.

**Comedy of Errors:** Director Václav Vorlíček and writer Miloš Macourek employ the classic comedy of errors format, using mistaken identities and physical transformations to create humor. The film explores what happens when two adult con artists are literally forced to start over as children—their schemes, their relationships, their entire sense of self are upended by their physical transformation.

**Social Satire:** Beyond its comedic surface, the film satirizes the beauty industry, scientific hubris, and the commodification of youth. The laboratory's work on regenerating cows being adapted for human vanity reflects Macourek's critical eye on consumer society and the lengths people will go to in pursuit of superficial ideals.

**Transformation and Identity:** The body-swap premise allows the film to explore questions of identity—are Zemánek and Liška still themselves when trapped in children's bodies? The film suggests that while physical appearance changes, core personality remains, and perhaps the most valuable qualities have nothing to do with age or appearance.

Což takhle dát si špenát remains a beloved entry in Czechoslovak comedy cinema, representing the creative partnership between Vorlíček and Macourek that produced several cult classics during the 1970s. The film's blend of science fiction premises with traditional Czech comedic sensibilities creates a unique entry in Eastern European science fiction cinema.

entries.title: Cyber City Oedo 808
entries.creator: Yoshiaki Kawajiri (Director), Madhouse (Studio)
entries.pitch: In the year 2808, the Japanese city of Oedo has descended into a dystopian nightmare where crime runs rampant and traditional law enforcement has failed. Desperate for a solution, the city government revives an ancient feudal practice known as hōmen—employing convicted criminals as law enforcement officers. Three heavily-sentenced prisoners are offered a chance at freedom: Sengoku, a hot-headed armed robber serving 375 years; Gogul, a brilliant computer hacker serving 310 years; and Benten, an androgynous assassin serving 295 years. Each wears an explosive collar around their neck that their commander can detonate at will, ensuring their obedience. Armed with traditional jitte weapons and paired with the robotic partner Varsus, these cyber-criminals are sent to hunt down the most dangerous digital criminals in the neon-soaked metropolis. Cyber City Oedo 808 is a gritty, action-packed cyberpunk masterpiece that predates many of the genre's most famous works, featuring exceptional animation, complex anti-heroes, and a haunting vision of Japan's distant future.
entries.truth:
The series unfolds across three interconnected episodes, each focusing on different cases that the three convicts must solve to reduce their sentences.

**Episode 1: The Sentenced (Sentenced)** - The introduction establishes the world and characters. Sengoku, Gogul, and Benten are recruited by the Cyber Police under Commander Juzo Hasegawa. Their first mission involves apprehending a rogue hacker who has been侵入ing corporate networks and causing massive disruptions across Oedo. The episode establishes the dynamics between the three convicts—Sengoku's reckless aggression, Gogul's technical expertise, and Benten's cold efficiency. They successfully capture the hacker but discover something disturbing: the criminal was merely a pawn in a larger conspiracy.

**Episode 2: The Bounty Hunter** - The team investigates a series of murders involving elite corporate executives. The killer appears to be a cybernetic super-soldier from a forgotten government project. As they dig deeper, they uncover evidence of illegal body modification programs and genetic engineering. Benten forms an unexpected connection with the target, leading to a climactic confrontation that forces the team to question their own humanity. The episode explores themes of identity and what it means to be human in a world where flesh and machine merge seamlessly.

**Episode 3: The Vampire (Vampire)** - The final and most acclaimed episode introduces a vampire-like entity that has been terrorizing Oedo's underground. This ancient being has adapted to the cyberpunk world, using technology to enhance its predatory capabilities. The team must work together to stop this creature while confronting their own darker natures. The episode features the series' most action-packed sequence and its most emotionally resonant climax, culminating in a bittersweet ending that defines the entire series.

Throughout all three episodes, a larger conspiracy emerges—the Cyber Police itself may be more corrupt than the criminals they hunt, and the convicts must decide whether to continue serving their sentence-reducing missions or expose the truth at great personal cost.
entries.core:
Cyber City Oedo 808 explores several themes that would become staples of the cyberpunk genre:

**Redemption Through Service**: The series questions whether criminals can ever truly redeem themselves. The protagonists are given a chance to earn their freedom through service, but at what cost to their souls? Their collars represent both literal and figurative leashes—the government doesn't just want their obedience, it wants to break their spirits.

**The Fusion of Old and New**: The series uniquely blends feudal Japanese concepts (the hōmen system, jitte weapons) with futuristic cyberpunk technology. This creates a distinctive aesthetic that feels both ancient and alien. The title "Oedo" (a historical name for Tokyo) emphasizes this collision of past and future.

**Humanity in the Machine Age**: The series probes questions of identity and humanity. What does it mean to be human when you can replace your body parts with superior cybernetics? The characters—including the antagonists—struggle with these questions, and the series offers no easy answers.

**Institutional Corruption**: The Cyber Police, presented as the "good guys," are revealed to be just another corrupt institution. This cynical view of authority would become a cyberpunk staple, reflecting real-world distrust of government and corporate power.

**Found Family Among Outcasts**: Despite their criminal backgrounds, the three convicts develop genuine bonds with each other and with their handler Kyōko. They are all societal rejects who find meaning in their missions and each other—a classic cyberpunk theme of finding family in the margins.

Yoshiaki Kawajiri (later known for directing *Ninja Scroll*) delivers a masterclass in compact storytelling, packing complex themes and character development into three 45-minute episodes. The series remains a cult classic not despite but because of its brevity—every scene serves a purpose, every moment earns its place.

entries.title: Cyberpunks
entries.creator: Mutation (developer), Core Design (publisher)
entries.pitch: Cyberpunks is a top-down shooter developed by Mutation and published by Core Design in 1993 exclusively for the Amiga computer. The game places players in control of three characters simultaneously, tasking them with navigating hostile alien environments, shooting enemy forces, and locating door passes to progress through levels. Released during the height of the cyberpunk aesthetic's popularity in media, Cyberpunks represents a fascinating period piece of early 1990s British game development, combining the twin-stick shooter mechanics popular at the time with themes of corporate warfare and artificial intelligence. The game was developed by the relatively obscure British studio Mutation, with Adrian Robert Cummings handling design, art, and music—a notable feat of single-developer ambition for the era.
entries.truth:
The player controls a squad of three cyberpunk operatives who must fight their way through alien-infested levels. Gameplay involves simultaneous control of all three characters, each with their own abilities and loadouts. The objective in each level is to eliminate hostile alien forces and locate door passes—key cards or access codes required to advance to subsequent areas. The game features multiple levels with increasing difficulty, requiring players to manage all three characters' positions and actions in real-time.

The narrative context, while minimal, establishes a world where megacorporations have developed advanced cybernetic soldiers to combat alien threats. The player characters are enhanced operatives—cyborg warriors with superior reflexes and firepower tasked with missions in hostile territories. The enemy forces are alien in origin, requiring the players to adapt to different combat scenarios as they progress. The game emphasizes action over story, with the plot serving primarily as a framework for the shooting mechanics rather than a deep narrative experience.
entries.core:
**Corporate Warfare and Cybernetic Enhancement:** The game reflects early 1990s anxieties about the intersection of corporate power and military technology. The megacorporations depicted as sending cyborg soldiers on dangerous missions embody concerns about private military forces operating beyond governmental oversight. The enhancement of human soldiers with cybernetic implants positions the game within the broader cyberpunk theme of human-machine fusion and the blurring of boundaries between organic and artificial.

**Simultaneous Multi-Character Control:** The game's most distinctive mechanical feature—controlling three characters at once—represents both a technical achievement for the Amiga platform and a design philosophy that rewards strategic multitasking. Players must coordinate all three operatives, positioning them optimally and managing their individual health and ammunition resources. This mechanic was relatively rare in 1990s shooters and demonstrates Mutation's ambition to create something beyond a typical run-and-gun experience.

**Amiga Era Preservation:** Cyberpunks exists as a time capsule of mid-1990s British gaming culture. The Amiga platform was particularly popular in Europe, especially in the UK, and Core Design was a prominent publisher known for titles like the early Tomb Raider games. The collaboration between the small studio Mutation and established publisher Core Design represents a moment in gaming history before the consolidation of the industry into fewer, larger publishers. The game's existence documents the diversity of creative voices active in that era.

entries.title: Dallos
entries.creator: Hisayuki Toriumi (Concept), Mamoru Oshii (Co-director)
entries.pitch: Dallos is a groundbreaking 4-episode OVA released in 1983, widely considered not only the first OVA ever produced but also the first animated direct-to-video release in history. Set in a near-future Earth where resources have been depleted, the story follows the colonists living on Moon mining stations who have been exploited by Earth's government for generations. When the colonists begin to resist through acts of terrorism, a young man named Shun Nonomura finds himself caught between loyalty to his people and the violent path they're taking. At the center of the colonists' hope is a mysterious artifact called Dallos—a structure on the Moon that has become an object of worship. This is a story about colonial oppression, generational divides, and the question of what it means to be human when your home is no longer your own.
entries.truth:
In the near future, humanity has exhausted Earth's natural resources. To survive, the Earth Federal Government established mining colonies on the Moon, where generations of colonists have been forced to labor under harsh conditions, sending vital resources back to Earth while living in indentured servitude.

The story opens by introducing the harsh reality of life on the Moon colony. The colonists, born and raised on the Moon, have never known Earth as anything but a distant oppressor. The Earth Federal Government treats them as second-class citizens, extracting wealth while providing minimal support. Tensions have been building for generations.

Shun Nonomura is a young colonist who becomes increasingly aware of the injustice faced by his people. He watches as younger colonists—natural-born Moon dwellers—begin to question the older generation's submission to Earth. The younger generation sees Earth not as a motherland but as an oppressive force that has stolen their birthright.

The colonists begin organizing acts of resistance against their Earth overseers. These terrorist acts escalate the conflict between the colony and Earth's government. The Earth Federal Government responds with increased security and crackdowns, creating a vicious cycle of violence.

At the heart of the colonists' hope is Dallos—a mysterious ancient structure on the Moon surface. The colonists have begun worshipping Dallos as a symbol of their resistance and as proof that their world has meaning beyond serving Earth. The structure represents both their cultural identity and their defiance.

Shun becomes deeply involved in the rebellion, his actions dramatically affecting those close to him—including his family and friends. He must navigate the generational divide within the colony itself: the older colonists remember Earth with some nostalgia and hope for peaceful resolution, while the younger generation has known only oppression and demands violent revolution.

The OVA explores the political and personal dimensions of this conflict. Characters must choose sides, and the consequences of those choices ripple through relationships and communities. The mystery of Dallos itself—what it truly is and whether it holds any supernatural significance—remains ambiguous, serving more as a focal point for the colonists' hopes and beliefs.
entries.core:
Dallos is a remarkably political work for its time, exploring themes of colonialism, generational conflict, and identity. The Moon colony functions as a metaphor for any marginalized community that has been exploited by a distant power—the colonists are essentially a conquered people, their labor extracted for the benefit of those who have never set foot on their soil.

The generational divide within the colony reflects real-world tensions in post-colonial societies. The older generation, with memories of Earth or at least stories of it, maintains some connection to the "motherland." The younger generation, born entirely on the Moon, sees themselves as a separate people with their own identity and their own right to self-determination.

Mamoru Oshii's involvement is significant—he would go on to become one of anime's most acclaimed directors, known for works like Ghost in the Shell. Dallos shows the early emergence of his mature concerns: political commentary, ambiguity in narrative, and characters caught in insoluble situations.

The mysterious artifact at the film's center raises questions about religion and resistance—whether hope needs to be grounded in reality, or whether the mere act of believing in something larger than oneself can be transformative for a oppressed people.

As the first OVA, Dallos also represents a pivotal moment in anime history. The direct-to-video format would become enormous influential, allowing for more experimental and mature content than theatrical releases typically permitted.

entries.title: Death Machine
entries.creator: Stephen Norrington (Director/Writer)
entries.pitch: In 2003, Chaank Armaments is the world's leading weapons manufacturer. When a cybernetic supersoldier called "Hard Man" goes on a murderous rampage, the corporation tries to cover up the incident. Hayden Cale, the new chief executive, demands transparency—but when she discovers the dark secrets hidden in the company's Vault 10, she becomes the target of the deranged weapon designer Jack Dante. With eco-warriors infiltrating the building and a deadly creation called the "Warbeast" on the loose, Cale must survive the night against both the corporate conspirators and her own creation. Featuring Brad Dourif in an unhinged performance as the villain, this obscure British-Japanese co-production is a cult sci-fi horror that was banned in multiple countries for its excessive violence.
entries.truth:
The film opens with the Hard Man, a cybernetic supersoldier, malfunctioning and massacring patrons in a diner before being detained. Public outcry follows, targeting the new CEO Hayden Cale. Chairman Scott Ridley attempts to cover up the incident and numerous issues with Project: Hard Man.

Cale demands full disclosure and wants Jack Dante, the weapon designer behind Hard Man, fired. Dante threatens Cale with blackmail, revealing he knows intimate details about her life. Meanwhile, three eco-warriors infiltrate Chaank headquarters to destroy the company's digital assets.

After Ridley is killed by something from Vault 10, Cale investigates and discovers the truth. She terminates Dante's employment and seals the vault—but Dante activates his invention: the Frontline Morale Destroyer, nicknamed "Warbeast," which kills one of the eco-warriors.

Dante broadcasts his demands, wanting reinstatement and "regular interface" with Cale. The survivors suit up one of the eco-warriors with Hard Man technology and data. A brutal fight ensues against the Warbeast, with casualties mounting.

In the climax, Dante is trapped in Vault 10 with his own creation—the Warbeast—which begins hunting him down. Cale and the remaining survivor escape as Dante meets his demise at the hands of his weapon.
entries.core: Death Machine explores the dangers of unchecked corporate power and the moral bankruptcy of the military-industrial complex. The film critiques how corporations hide behind national security to commit atrocities, and how weapons designers become detached from humanity. The character of Dante represents the archetypal mad scientist—brilliant but psychologically unhinged, creating death machines without regard for human life. The film also touches on corporate espionage, whistleblowing, and the price of technological advancement. With its Cyberpunk aesthetic and practical effects, it stands as a relic of early 90s sci-fi horror—a time when filmmakers could still explore dark technological themes without the CGI saturation that would later dominate the genre.

entries.title: Death Watch
entries.creator: Directed by Bertrand Tavernier; Based on "The Unsleeping Eye" (1973) by David G. Compton
entries.pitch: In a near-future where death from illness has become rare, Katherine Mortenhoe is diagnosed with an incurable disease and becomes an unlikely celebrity. A ruthless television executive offers her enormous money to allow her final days to be filmed as a reality show called "Death Watch" - but she doesn't realize the man helping her escape is actually a covert cameraman with cameras implanted behind his eyes. This French gem from director Bertrand Tavernier (Round Midnight, A Sunday in the Country) is a chilling meditation on privacy, media exploitation, and the voyeuristic nature of audiences.
entries.truth:
The film opens in a world where terminal illness has become exceedingly rare, making dying celebrities rare and valuable. When Katherine Mortenhoe receives a terminal diagnosis, she becomes the first "dying celebrity" in years - and television networks see pound signs.

Vincent Ferriman, a top executive at the NTV network, approaches Katherine with an offer: £300,000 to allow her final days to be filmed as the ultimate reality show, "Death Watch." Unbeknownst to Katherine, they've already secretly recorded her being told of her diagnosis, as her doctor has been colluding with NTV (giving her "painkillers" that actually make her progressively sicker).

Accepting the money and going on the run, Katherine disguises herself and seeks refuge in a night shelter, where she meets Roddy - who she believes is just a helpful stranger. In reality, Roddy is a senior NTV cameraman who has undergone an experimental surgical procedure: cameras and transmitters have been implanted behind his eyes, allowing everything he sees to be broadcast live to NTV's editing team. He's doing this for the money to support his estranged wife and son. The catch: if he experiences darkness for too long, he'll go blind - so he must keep himself awake with a small flashlight pointed at his eyes.

As Katherine and Roddy travel together toward her goal of seeing her first husband Gerald before she dies, the audience knows what Katherine does not. Roddy struggles with his conscience as he grows closer to Katherine. He eventually discovers the primetime broadcast of "Death Watch" and has an emotional breakdown, throwing his essential flashlight into the water. Katherine finds him, but it's too late - he's gone blind.

When Vincent arrives with a film crew by helicopter, Katherine has reached the end. Despite Gerald's pleas, she takes all her remaining pills and dies. Vincent reveals that she's not actually dying - the "medicine" was making her sick, but it's too late. Gerald threatens Vincent, who leaves with Roddy's wife to care for her blind husband.
entries.core:
Death Watch is a hauntingly prescient film that predicted reality television decades before shows like Big Brother or The Bachelor made voyeurism mainstream entertainment. The film explores how society transforms dying - one of humanity's most private and sacred moments - into content for consumption.

Tavernier examines the ethics of media manipulation: Katherine is exploited at her most vulnerable moment, with her doctor bribed to poison her for better ratings. The film asks uncomfortable questions about what we as audiences actually want: genuine human experience, or curated tragedy performed for our benefit?

The film also explores isolation and authenticity. Both Katherine and Roddy are running from their lives - she from death, he from his family - yet both find unexpected connection. The contrast between Katherine's genuine dying and the manufactured drama of Death Watch creates a poignant commentary on truth versus performance.

Visually, the film is striking - much of it shot in Glasgow, using the Necropolis and Cathedral to create a moody, elegiac atmosphere. The performances are excellent, particularly Romy Schneider as the dignified, confused Katherine and Harvey Keitel as the conflicted, ultimately compassionate Roddy.

entries.title: Deicide 3: Distorted Existence
entries.creator: Studio Jacobin
entries.pitch:
Deicide 3: Distorted Existence is a cinematic Korean adventure game from 1997 that stands as a remarkable artifact of early Korean game development. Set in a dystopian cyberpunk future, the game follows Kim Chang-ki, a 34-year-old ex-SWAT captain who has been transferred to the Public Security Department directly under the Presidential Security Office. Developed by Studio Jacobin and published by Bisco Co., Ltd., this title represents one of the most ambitious Korean-produced games of its era, featuring hand-drawn anime-style graphics and a complex narrative that draws clear inspiration from classic Japanese adventure games like Snatcher.

The game combines point-and-click adventure mechanics with shooting sequences, creating an experience that feels distinctly Asian while tackling themes familiar to the cyberpunk genre. Its visual presentation, entirely rendered in detailed anime artistry, creates an immersive atmosphere that was relatively rare for Korean productions of the mid-1990s. The narrative delves into themes of government surveillance, corporate power, and personal identity within a technologically advanced but socially fractured society.
entries.truth:
The player assumes the role of Kim Chang-ki, an experienced SWAT operative who has been reassigned to the Public Security Department—a government agency operating directly under the Presidential Security Office. This transfer is not a promotion but rather a strategic repositioning, as Kim finds himself navigating the complex political landscape of a dystopian Korea in the year 2024.

The gameplay unfolds as a detective story, requiring players to interrogate NPCs, explore various locations throughout the cyberpunk metropolis, and gather evidence to solve the mystery at the heart of the narrative. Kim's toolkit includes standard detective equipment: a knife, ID card, a tablet-style digital diary (which also serves as the game's save system), mobile phone, coins, and a bank card. As the investigation progresses, players will acquire additional items and evidence.

The game features multiple gameplay modes. The adventure segments involve mouse-based interaction, with players choosing actions like talking to characters, examining objects, or using items when hovering over interactable elements. An inventory system, accessible via a button in the upper-right corner, allows players to manage their collected items without size limitations. Additionally, the game incorporates action-oriented shooting sequences that demand precision and quick reflexes.

The narrative explores themes of governmental overreach, the blurred lines between human and machine in a cybernetic age, and the struggle for truth and justice within a corrupt system. Kim must navigate the treacherous waters of political intrigue while uncovering darker secrets that probe fundamental questions about consciousness, identity, and the nature of humanity itself.
entries.core:
**Government Control and Surveillance:** The setting presents a society where the government exercises pervasive control over its citizens through the Public Security Department. This theme resonates with broader cyberpunk concerns about the erosion of privacy and the concentration of power in the hands of a few, reflecting anxieties specific to Korea's historical relationship with authoritarian governance.

**Identity and Transformation:** As a former SWAT officer now working within a suspicious government apparatus, Kim Chang-ki embodies the theme of identity crisis. His transition from one role to another, combined with the cyberpunk setting's inherent questions about human augmentation, creates a narrative that explores what it means to maintain one's integrity and moral compass when surrounded by corruption.

**Detective Fiction in a Cyberpunk Frame:** The game draws heavily from the visual novel and adventure game traditions, particularly the legacy of Snatcher. This influence is evident in its narrative structure, which combines investigative gameplay with a dystopian setting—merging the noir detective genre with cyberpunk's technological anxieties to create something distinctively Korean.

**Korean Game Development Ambition:** Deicide 3 represents a significant moment in Korean gaming history, demonstrating that domestic developers could produce narrative-driven, visually sophisticated games that rivaled Japanese and Western productions. Its existence challenges the narrative that the Korean game industry only emerged globally in the MMORPG era, showcasing earlier ambitions in the adventure game genre.

The game's emphasis on narrative complexity and atmospheric world-building positions it as a cult classic within the cyberpunk adventure genre, remembered for its ambition, visual style, and its place as a pioneering Korean contribution to interactive storytelling.

entries.title: Delpower X Bakuhatsu Miracle Genki!
entries.creator: Masato Sato (Director), Big Bang (Studio)
entries.pitch: Delpower X Bakuhatsu Miracle Genki! is a quirky 1986 mecha OVA from the obscure Japanese studio Big Bang. The story centers on Hanegi Manami, a teenage girl who discovers her grandfather's specially designed powered armor suit, Del Power X, which was built specifically for her. Unlike typical mecha series featuring giant robots or serious military conflicts, this OVA takes a lighthearted, comedic approach to the powered armor genre. The 41-minute runtime delivers a compact, feel-good narrative about a young woman coming into her own through an unexpected encounter with advanced technology. The voice cast includes several notable seiyuu like Hiromi Tsuru and Ryo Horikawa, lending talent to this lesser-known entry in the 1980s OVA catalog.
entries.truth:
The plot follows Hanegi Manami, an ordinary Japanese teenage girl going about her daily life. One day, a helicopter arrives delivering a mysterious package for her grandfather—a powered armor suit called Del Power X, designed specifically for Hanegi Manami herself. The suit represents cutting-edge technology and was apparently created with her in mind, though the exact reasons are part of the OVA's narrative.

As Manami learns to operate the Del Power X, she finds herself drawn into situations where the powered armor becomes useful. The OVA explores her growth and adaptation to this unexpected responsibility, balancing comedic sequences with moments of genuine character development. The supporting cast includes various colorful characters who interact with Manami as she discovers what it means to be chosen for something larger than herself.

The story maintains a optimistic tone throughout, avoiding the darker themes common in many 1980s mecha series. Instead, it focuses on the personal journey of a young protagonist discovering her potential and the unexpected connections between technology and individual growth.
entries.core:
**Youth and Unexpected Responsibility:** The OVA centers on the theme of a young person being thrust into a situation beyond their expectations. Manami represents ordinary youth suddenly confronted with extraordinary circumstances, exploring how young people adapt to responsibilities they never sought.

**Technology and Personal Connection:** Unlike typical mecha series where giant robots serve military or combat purposes, Del Power X is a personal suit designed for one specific individual. This creates a more intimate relationship between protagonist and technology, suggesting tools can be extensions of personal identity rather than weapons of destruction.

**Comedy in the Mecha Genre:** The OVA stands as an example of lighter, comedic mecha storytelling that emerged in the 1980s OVA market. It represents the diversity within the mecha genre, showing that stories about powered suits didn't always need to be serious or militaristic.

**1980s OVA Culture:** The mid-1980s were a peak period for original video animations in Japan, with studios producing experimental and niche content outside the television broadcast system. Delpower X exemplifies this era of creative freedom, where studios like Big Bang could produce unique, smaller-scale works that wouldn't fit mainstream television formats.

The OVA remains a footnote in anime history, representing the vast creative experimentation occurring in Japan's 1980s OVA boom—works that often received limited distribution but contributed to the medium's diverse artistic landscape.

entries.title: Demon City Shinjuku
entries.creator: Directed by Yoshiaki Kawajiri; Based on the novel by Hideyuki Kikuchi; Studio: Madhouse
entries.pitch: In 1990s Tokyo, a catastrophic "Demon Quake" destroys Shinjuku, transforming it into a demon-haunted wasteland. Ten years later, the city remains a twisted netherworld where supernatural entities roam the ruined skyscrapers. When the World President is targeted by a curse, his daughter recruits Kyoya Izayoi—the son of a legendary hero who died fighting the demon lord Rebi Ra—to venture into the heart of darkness. What awaits is a surreal journey through a city where the boundary between human and monster has dissolved completely.
entries.truth:
The story opens with the epic battle between former friends: the heroic Genichirou and his corrupted former ally Rebi Ra, who has allowed himself to be possessed by demonic forces to gain ultimate power. Rebi Ra summons a devastating earthquake that tears Shinjuku from the rest of Tokyo, transforming it into a demon realm. Genichirou is killed, and Shinjuku becomes a twisted wilderness of monsters and ruined architecture.

Ten years later, the World President—who was saved years ago by the hero Aguni Rai—is attacked by cursed plants. The truth emerges: Rebi Ra is using the President as a ritual sacrifice to fully open the gateway between Earth and the Demon Realm. The President has only three days to live unless Rebi Ra is defeated.

The President's daughter, Sayaka Rama, seeks out the only person who can help: Kyoya Izayoi, the son of Genichirou, who has inherited his father's supernatural powers. Initially reluctant, Kyoya is persuaded to enter Demon City Shinjuku after Sayaka pleads for his help.

Inside the ruined city, Kyoya encounters bizarre demons, surreal landscapes, and strange allies. He battles through various demonic forces, learning about his father's legacy and his own destiny. The city itself is a character—ruined buildings, neon signs flickering through the chaos, streets that lead to impossible places.

The climax reveals that Chiaki, Kyoya's friend, has been working with the demons, and Kyoya must face both the external threat of Rebi Ra and the internal betrayal. In the final confrontation, Kyoya uses his father's legacy and his own courage to defeat Rebi Ra, closing the portal and potentially saving both the President and the world.

The OVA ends with theDemon Quake reversed, Shinjuku returning to normal, and Kyoya becoming the new protector of Tokyo.
entries.core:
Demon City Shinjuku is a fascinating fusion of cyberpunk urban decay and supernatural horror, exploring themes of identity, legacy, and the monstrous. The film poses uncomfortable questions about what makes a monster: is it appearance, or is it choices? The demons in Shinjuku are often more human in their motivations than their human adversaries.

The setting itself is a powerful metaphor for urban alienation and the loss of humanity in modern society. Shinjuku becomes a physical manifestation of the darkness that lurks beneath the surface of civilization—the part of the city we pretend doesn't exist.

The film also explores legacy and generational trauma. Kyoya must come to terms with his father's legacy while forging his own identity. He's not simply repeating his father's heroic path but finding his own way.

Visually, the film is a stunning example of late-80s anime artistry. The contrast between the gleaming modern Tokyo and the ruined demon city creates a powerful visual metaphor. The action sequences are choreographed with precision, and the demon designs are genuinely unsettling.

The film was notably influential—the opening fight scene was reused in the 1995 cyberpunk film Johnny Mnemonic. However, the OVA remains relatively unknown outside of anime collector circles, making it a true hidden gem of the cyberpunk genre.

entries.title: Dobaaraa
entries.creator: Anurag Kashyap
entries.pitch:
Dobaaraa (transl. "Second Time") is a 2022 Indian Hindi-language science fiction mystery thriller directed by Anurag Kashyap, serving as an official remake of the 2018 Spanish film Mirage. The film stars Taapsee Pannu as Antara Awasthi, a woman who discovers an opportunity to save the life of a 12-year-old boy who accidentally witnessed a death during a thunderstorm 25 years ago. The key to her mission lies in connecting with the past through a television set during a similar storm in the present day.

The narrative unfolds through a complex web of parallel timelines and temporal anomalies. When Antara encounters the phenomenon, she finds herself able to communicate across time, setting off a chain of events that threatens to unravel reality itself. The film is notable for its intricate storytelling, requiring viewers to pay close attention to the constant shifts between timelines—a hallmark of Kashyap's signature style that has earned praise from critics for its bold narrative construction.

Despite receiving highly positive reviews from critics who lauded its direction, screenplay, and performances, Dobaaraa was a commercial failure at the Indian box office, collecting only around ₹6.77 crore against its ₹30 crore budget. The film opened the London Indian Film Festival 2022 before its theatrical release on August 19, 2022.
entries.truth:
The story centers on Antara Awasthi (Taapsee Pannu), who in 2022 finds herself living a complicated life—estranged from her husband Vikas (Rahul Bhat) and working as a doctor. During a violent thunderstorm, she discovers that her television set has somehow connected to a signal from 1992, allowing her to communicate with a young boy named Anay Anand (Aarrian Sawant). Through a series of blurred television transmissions, Antara realizes that Anay had accidentally witnessed a murder 25 years ago—a murder that shaped the lives of everyone in the present.

The central mystery involves the death of a man named Abdul, which Anay witnessed as a child. Through her connection to the past via the television, Antara attempts to warn Anay and prevent the tragedy, believing that saving the boy will somehow improve her own circumstances in the present. However, as she manipulates the timeline, she discovers that every change creates unexpected consequences—alternate realities where her relationships, her career, and even her very identity are fundamentally altered.

The film's narrative operates on multiple temporal layers, with Antara desperately trying to piece together the puzzle of what happened in 1992 while simultaneously grappling with the realization that meddling with the past may have irreversible effects on the present. The mystery deepens as she works alongside a police officer with a mysterious past (Pavail Gulati), uncovering secrets about the original crime and its connection to her own family.

Kashyap's direction creates a disorienting but engaging atmosphere, constantly shifting between timelines in a manner that demands viewer attention. The film's climax reveals the full complexity of its time-bending premise, showing how Antara's attempts to rewrite history create a reality that is both familiar and profoundly alien—much like the original Spanish source material, but with distinctively Indian emotional beats.
entries.core:
**Time Travel as Narrative Puzzle:** Dobaaraa explores the paradoxes of time travel through the lens of cause and effect, examining how attempts to alter the past inevitably reshape the present in unpredictable ways. The film engages with the "butterfly effect" concept, demonstrating that even well-intentioned interventions in historical events can lead to devastating unintended consequences. The television set serves as both a literal device and a metaphor—representing how we receive and interpret information from the past, and how media shapes our understanding of historical events.

**Isolation and Individual Agency:** Antara's journey is fundamentally one of isolation—she is the only person who remembers the original timeline, making her both uniquely powerful and profoundly alone. The film explores the psychological toll of carrying knowledge that no one else possesses, and the desperation that comes with believing one person can change fate. Her isolation is compounded by the feeling of being trapped in a reality that feels both familiar and wrong—a commentary on how trauma can make the present feel unreal.

**Memory and Identity:** The film interrogates the nature of memory and how our past experiences define our identities. When the timeline shifts, Antara finds that her relationships, her career, and even her own personality have been altered—raising questions about whether we are defined by our memories or by some deeper essence. The 25-year gap between timelines also reflects on generational memory and how past traumas echo through time, affecting people who weren't even alive when the original events occurred.

**Critical Reception and Artistic Merit:** While Dobaaraa struggled commercially, critics praised its intellectual ambition and complex storytelling. The film holds a 73% rating on Rotten Tomatoes, with reviewers highlighting Kashyap's ability to adapt the intricate original material for Indian audiences. Some critics noted that the film requires multiple viewings to fully appreciate its temporal complexity—a characteristic that places it within the tradition of cerebral science fiction that rewards engaged, attentive viewing.

**The Remake Question:** As an official remake of Mirage, Dobaaraa exists in dialogue with its Spanish predecessor. While the core narrative remains similar, Kashyap infuses the film with distinctively Indian emotional textures and cultural references. The film can be seen as exploring how universal themes of time, memory, and regret translate across cultural contexts—demonstrating that the anxiety of being unable to change one's past is a universally human experience.

entries.title: Domu: A Child's Dream
entries.creator: Katsuhiro Otomo
entries.pitch: Domu is a psychological horror manga by Katsuhiro Otomo, creator of Akira, set in a sprawling Tokyo public housing complex called the Tsutsumi Housing Complex. When a series of mysterious suicides and violent deaths rocks the apartment block, two detectives investigate and discover a terrifying truth: an elderly man named Old Cho, a senile resident with powerful psychic abilities, is responsible. As the death toll rises, a young girl named Etsuko moves into the complex—and it becomes clear she is the only one with powers strong enough to stop Old Cho. First serialized in 1980-1981, Domu was the first manga to win the prestigious Nihon SF Taisho Award (Japan Science Fiction Grand Prix) and won the Seiun Award for Best Comic. It predates Otomo's more famous Akira and showcases his talent for blending urban horror with themes of generational conflict and psychic warfare.
entries.truth:
The story begins with Mr. Ueno, a tenant at the Tsutsumi Housing Complex, jumping to his death from a building roof—the latest in a string of thirty-two mysterious deaths over just three years. Inspectors Yamagawa and Takayama investigate, initially dismissed as suicides, but soon discover something far more sinister. The case takes an even darker turn when a patrolman at the complex suddenly jumps to his death, this time with his service pistol missing from his belt.

As Yamagawa delves deeper, he interviews the complex manager and learns about several troubled residents: Mrs. Tezuka, sent to a mental hospital after a miscarriage; Little Yo, a mentally disabled man falsely accused of child molestation; and Old Cho, a senile old man living alone. When Yamagawa returns to the complex to investigate further, he experiences a terrifying encounter—his pager explodes, and he is pursued by an unseen force with psychic powers. He is found dead on the ground below the building.

The arrival of young Etsuko and her family changes everything. When Old Cho attempts to drop a baby from a balcony, Etsuko instinctively uses her own powerful psychic abilities to save the child. She realizes that Old Cho is behind all the deaths—he has been using his telekinetic powers to manipulate victims into killing themselves and collecting trophies from each one. Old Cho, once a quiet old man, has become a dangerous psychic predator, using his powers to exact twisted justice on residents he deems "sinful."

A brutal game of cat and mouse ensues between Old Cho and Etsuko across the apartment complex. Old Cho possesses various victims, including a young man who slits his own throat in front of Etsuko, and an abusive alcoholic whom he arms with the dead patrolman's pistol. Etsuko must use all her courage and psychic strength to protect the other residents and ultimately confront Old Cho in a climactic psychic battle.
entries.core:
**Psychic Powers and Generational Conflict:** At its heart, Domu explores the conflict between generations through the lens of psychic ability. Old Cho represents the bitter, resentful elderly—marginalized, forgotten, and seething with accumulated grudges against a society that has dismissed him. His psychic powers, awakened or intensified by his senility, become a weapon of revenge against a world he perceives as having wronged him. Etsuko, by contrast, represents innocent youth, her powers emerging naturally and used defensively to protect life rather than destroy it. The clash between them is not just physical but philosophical: what happens when the powerless gain extraordinary power?

**Urban Isolation and Alienation:** Otomo sets the horror within a massive anonymous apartment complex, a perfect metaphor for modern urban isolation. Residents live side by side yet remain strangers; the elderly especially are forgotten, their suffering unnoticed until violence erupts. The Tsutsumi Housing Complex becomes a character in itself—a labyrinthine setting of corridors and shared walls where privacy is nonexistent but genuine connection is rare. This mirrors Otomo's own inspiration: the apartment complex he lived in when first moving to Tokyo, and news reports of suicides at similar housing developments.

**Horror and the Mundane:** Domu stands out for grounding its supernatural horror in everyday details. The police investigations, welfare checks, neighbor gossip, and domestic scenes of children playing create a stark contrast with the psychic violence. The horror doesn't occur in some fantastical otherworld but in recognizable spaces—a rooftop, a courtyard, a child's bedroom. This approach, which Otomo would later refine in Akira, treats the extraordinary with the same observational detail as the mundane.

**Precursor to Akira:** Reading Domu immediately before Akira, one sees Otomo developing many themes that would define his later masterpiece: the apocalyptic potential of psychic powers, the tension between individual ability and collective responsibility, and above all, Tokyo as a site of both technological modernity and social decay. But where Akira would become a sprawling epic of generational and political warfare, Domu remains focused and intimate—a contained horror story that achieves its devastating effects through restraint and psychological precision.

entries.title: Dreamweb
entries.creator: Creative Reality (Developer) / Empire Interactive (Publisher)
entries.pitch: Dreamweb is a 1994 point-and-click cyberpunk adventure game developed by the small UK studio Creative Reality and published by Empire Interactive. Set in a dystopian near-future England, the game puts players in the role of Ryan, a bartender who has been plagued by strange dreams of an entity known as the Dreamweb—a mystical force that recruits humans to eliminate "seven evils" threatening to break loose and plunge humanity into chaos. What makes Dreamweb distinctive is its unflinching embrace of mature themes: violence, brief full frontal nudity, and an overall tone that refuses to soften its edges. The game draws heavy visual and thematic influence from Ridley Scott's Blade Runner, while its narrative structure—centered on the concept of seven deadly sins—owes a debt to the 1986 film Highlander. Originally released for MS-DOS and Amiga, Dreamweb was controversial enough to be refused classification in Australia upon initial release due to its explicit content. The game has since faded into obscurity, remembered primarily by dedicated adventure game enthusiasts and those who appreciate the darker corners of 1990s gaming.
entries.truth:
The game opens with protagonist Ryan waking from another recurring dream of the Dreamweb—a mystical web-like entity that exists between dimensions. In these dreams, the "master monk" of the Keepers appears to Ryan, charging him with a mission: kill seven individuals known as the "seven evils" who are on the verge of uniting and breaking the Dreamweb, which would send humanity into irreversible chaos.

Ryan begins his journey in his everyday life as a bartender in a grim, rain-soaked dystopian city that appears to be somewhere in England (the use of British English terms like "lift" and "bonnet" confirms the UK setting). His first target is David Crane, a rock star staying at a local hotel. After obtaining a gun through his friend Louis, Ryan infiltrates the hotel, kills Crane and two bodyguards, and is transported to the Dreamweb where he learns of his second target: a military general named Sterling.

Ryan's journey continues as he tracks down and eliminates each of the seven evils. General Sterling is killed in a television studio when Ryan drops a heavy crate on him during a live talk show broadcast. The third target is Sartain, the owner of the company where Ryan's girlfriend Eden works—Ryan pursues him to a rooftop where he kills him with a hover car. The fourth target is Juliet Chappel, whom Ryan discovers in a destroyed house, though he initially believes her dead before learning her location leads to a church.

Ryan's fifth target is Diane Underwood, found at a heavily guarded beach house. Ryan sabotages the security system by dousing the control box in water, causing the security turret to go haywire and kill Underwood. The sixth target is Father O'Rourke, found dead in the secret underground passages beneath a church—leaving only one final evil: Dr. Beckett.

In the climax, Ryan locates Beckett in the subway system, linked to the church's underground passages. As Beckett tries to flee, he is run over by a train. After completing all seven kills, Ryan returns to the Dreamweb one last time, where the master monk thanks him—but also reveals Ryan's fate. Returning to the real world for the final time, Ryan is shot dead by police for his numerous murders. His last vision is of his soul entering the Dreamweb, suggesting either transcendence or descent into madness—the game intentionally leaves this ambiguous.

A prequel diary included with the game, "Diary of a (Mad?) Man," strongly implies that Ryan may have fabricated the entire Dreamweb scenario as a symptom of psychosis—suggesting the entire game may represent a psychotic break rather than actual supernatural events.
entries.core:
**Ambiguity of Reality and Madness:** Dreamweb presents a fundamental question about the nature of Ryan's mission: is the Dreamweb a real supernatural entity, or is Ryan simply descending into madness? The included "Diary of a (Mad?) Man" deliberately undermines the player assumptions, suggesting the entire adventure may be a psychotic episode. This ambiguity places the game in dialogue with films like Fight Club and Jacob's Ladder, where the line between reality and delusion blurs. The thematic choice to leave this unresolved creates a disturbing undercurrent: even as players complete the "heroic" mission, they must confront the possibility that they have been murders committed by an complicit in multiple unreliable narrator.

**Violence as Catharsis and Commentary:** Unlike most adventure games of the era, Dreamweb places no moral weight on killing. Ryan murders seven people throughout the game, often in graphic ways—shooting bodyguards, dropping a man to his death on live television, destroying a hover car with its occupant inside. The game neither celebrates nor condemns this violence; it simply presents it. This approach was groundbreaking (and controversial) in 1994, presaging later games that would explore the psychological cost of violence. The designers seemed to be asking: what does it mean to be an instrument of cosmic "justice" when that justice requires becoming a murderer?

**Cyberpunk Aesthetics and British Identity:** While Dreamweb clearly owes debts to Blade Runner's visual language—similar typography, the white-on-black credits, the rain-soaked dystopian city—it grounds these familiar cyberpunk tropes in a specifically British context. The use of UK English, the grey urban environments, and the localized references all serve to translate the American cyberpunk aesthetic into a British framework. This made Dreamweb a unique entry in the cyberpunk gaming canon—neither a Hollywood production nor a Japanese import, but a distinctly British take on the genre.

**The Self-Destructive Quest:** The game's ending—where Ryan completes his mission only to be killed by police—embodies a tragic structure. The "hero" achieves his cosmic purpose but is destroyed by the mundane consequences of his actions. This mirrors the Highlander influence, where "there can be only one" leads to pyrrhic victories. Ryan's final fate suggests that fulfilling a cosmic mission does not guarantee redemption or survival; sometimes doing the "right" thing still leads to destruction.

entries.title: Dünyayı Kurtaran Adam
entries.creator: Çetin İnanç (Director), Cüneyt Arkın (Writer/Star)
entries.pitch: Dünyayı Kurtaran Adam (literally "The Man Who Saves the World") is a 1982 Turkish science fantasy martial arts superhero film that has achieved legendary cult status as "Turkish Star Wars." Directed by Çetin İnanç and starring the legendary Turkish actor Cüneyt Arkın, this low-budget wonder combines science fiction themes with martial arts action in a way that's more reminiscent of 1970s Hong Kong Golden Harvest films than George Lucas's space opera. The film is infamous for its unauthorized incorporation of footage, music, and sound effects from Star Wars and numerous other sci-fi classics—making it a fascinating artifact of Turkish "Remakesploitation" cinema. Despite being panned by critics and frequently listed among the worst films ever made, it has gained a devoted cult following and was even restored in 2016 when the only surviving 35mm print was discovered, leading to a Blu-ray release in 2021.
entries.truth:
The story follows two astronauts, Murat and Ali, who crash land on a desert alien planet following a battle in space (the battle is depicted through repurposed Star Wars footage and newsreel clips of Soviet and American rocket launches). The two heroes find themselves in a hostile environment filled with skeletons on horseback, deadly creatures, and the tyrannical rule of an evil Sorcerer.

As they explore this alien world, they discover it's ruled by a 1,000-year-old wizard who seeks to conquer Earth. The Sorcerer has already obtained what he calls "the power of Earth's ancestry" in the form of a golden brain, but he needs an actual human brain to bypass Earth's protective "shield of concentrated human brain molecules" (depicted through footage of the Death Star from Star Wars).

The heroes are captured and brought to a gladiatorial arena. The Sorcerer offers Murat a deal: join him and rule the cosmos. When Murat refuses, the Sorcerer captures his friends and family. Murat manages to escape and discovers a powerful sword forged by "the 13th clan"—a lightning bolt-shaped weapon that grants its wielder incredible power.

However, Ali becomes envious of the sword's power, betrays Murat, and hands the artifacts over to the Sorcerer. Ali is killed in the process. Grief-stricken but determined, Murat melts down the sword and golden brain to forge magical gauntlets and boots. Equipped with these super-powered accessories, he battles through monsters and skeletons, finally confronting and defeating the Sorcerer with a dramatic karate chop. He then returns to Earth in a ship resembling the Millennium Falcon.
entries.core:
**Cultural Appropriation as Creative Act:** Dünyayı Kurtaran Adam represents a fascinating case of cinematic collage as creative expression. By incorporating footage from Star Wars, Raiders of the Lost Ark, Flash Gordon, Battlestar Galactica, and numerous other films—along with John Williams' iconic scores—the film creates something entirely new from existing materials. This "Remakesploitation" approach reflects the resourcefulness of Turkish filmmakers working with limited budgets during the country's film industry boom of the 1970s and early 1980s.

**The Turkish Film Industry's Golden Age:** The film emerged during a prolific period in Turkish cinema, when local studios produced hundreds of films annually, often working with minimal budgets and maximum creativity. These "Turksploitation" films encompassed every genre—from detective stories to horror to superhero adventures. Dünyayı Kurtaran Adam represents the sci-fi branch of this phenomenon, blending the superhero genre (popularized by characters like Süpermen) with martial arts action that was trendy thanks to Hong Kong cinema imports.

**East Meets East:** Despite its "Turkish Star Wars" nickname, the film has more in common with Hong Kong martial arts films from Golden Harvest than with American space operas. The fight choreography, the fantasy action, and the hero's journey all reflect the influence of Bruce Lee and Jackie Chen-style films that were popular in Turkey during this era. This makes the film a unique hybrid—Turkish filmmakers imitating Hong Kong filmmakers imitating Hollywood.

**Cult Status and Redemption:** Initially dismissed as one of the worst films ever made, Dünyayı Kurtaran Adam has been rehabilitated through decades of cult appreciation. Film historian Ed Glaser's discovery of the only surviving 35mm print in 2016 led to a proper restoration, demonstrating that even "so-bad-it's-good" films can achieve canonical status. Today, it's celebrated as a beloved piece of cinematic history—a testament to the enduring appeal of earnest, ambitious filmmaking regardless of budget constraints.

**The Dream of Turkish Cinema:** At its heart, the film represents a bold attempt by Turkish filmmakers to compete on the international stage, even if the execution was technically flawed. The ambition to create a space epic—however improvised—reflects the creative ambition of Turkish cinema during its golden age, when local filmmakers weren't afraid to tackle any genre or subject matter, no matter how challenging the production constraints.

entries.title: Electronic Man 337
entries.creator: Director Im Jeong-gyu
entries.pitch:
