Yoshiura's animation style, with its distinctive color palette and dreamlike imagery, perfectly serves the contemplative narrative. Pale Cocoon is a brief but profound work that rewards careful viewing—it poses big questions about civilization, memory, and what we owe to the past.

entries.title: Parable of the Sower
entries.creator: Octavia E. Butler
entries.pitch: Set in a near-future post-apocalyptic United States ravaged by climate change and extreme inequality, Parable of the Sower follows Lauren Olamina, a young Black woman born with "hyper-empathy" - an ability to feel the physical sensations of others. When her gated community is destroyed by violent raiders, Lauren must journey northward through a landscape of chaos, poverty, and collapse, gathering survivors and spreading her new philosophy called Earthseed. What makes this novel remarkable is its fundamental optimism: despite the devastating worldbuilding, it's ultimately a story about hope, resilience, and humanity's potential to transcend its worst impulses by reaching for the stars.
entries.truth:
The novel takes place primarily in 2024-2027, as the United States has collapsed into chaos due to climate change, corporate greed, and massive wealth inequality. Lauren Oya Olamina lives in Robledo, California, a former affluent suburb now enclosed behind walls, surrounded by desperate poverty. Her mother abused drugs during pregnancy, leaving Lauren with Hyper-empathy Syndrome - she physically experiences the pain she witnesses in others.

Lauren keeps a journal chronicling the deteriorating situation. Her community struggles to survive as resources dwindle, while outside the walls, "pyros" (fire-raisers) and desperate hordes roam the land. Her younger half-brother Keith rebels against the community's safety and runs away, eventually being tortured and killed by a gang of thieves. Her father disappears during a work trip and is presumed dead.

In 2027, when Lauren is eighteen, the community is attacked and destroyed by a massive group of raiders. Most residents are murdered, including Lauren's family. She escapes with only two neighbors - Harry Balter, a former teacher, and Zahra Moss, a young mother. Disguised as a man for safety, Lauren begins traveling north toward Oregon, where she hopes to find work and stability.

The journey is perilous. The world outside has regressed to near-feudal conditions, with each town or region operating as its own fiefdom with strict borders. Money still exists but is nearly worthless. Lauren and her companions face constant danger from desperate travelers, corporate security forces, and the elements.

Along the way, they rescue others from various dangers: a woman named Nita, a man named Touby, and others. Lauren also begins sharing her philosophy - Earthseed - which she has been developing since childhood. Earthseed's central tenet is that "God is Change" and that humanity's destiny is to leave Earth and populate other worlds. She believes this gives humans purpose and something to strive for beyond mere survival.

The group eventually meets Dr. Andrew Bankole, a physician who reminds Lauren of her father. He joins their journey and becomes a key member of the community. The novel ends with Lauren and her growing group settling in a community in northern California, preparing to establish a new society based on Earthseed principles.
entries.core:
Parable of the Sower is fundamentally about hope in the face of apocalypse. While many post-apocalyptic stories focus on survival, collapse, or the darker aspects of human nature, Butler crafts a narrative that insists humanity can choose to build something better. Lauren's Earthseed philosophy represents a forward-looking, adaptable worldview that embraces change rather than fearing it.

The novel explores climate change and environmental collapse with startling prescience, depicting droughts, fires, and resource scarcity decades before these became mainstream concerns. It also examines systemic inequality, showing how climate devastation disproportionately affects the poor and marginalized.

Butler's depiction of community formation is particularly compelling. Lauren doesn't just gather survivors - she creates a new social fabric based on mutual aid, shared purpose, and inclusive values. The novel suggests that the collapse of old institutions creates space for new, better ones to emerge.

The "hyper-empathy" ability serves as a metaphor for compassion and interconnectedness. Lauren's condition forces her to feel others' pain, but she transforms this into a source of strength - understanding others deeply allows her to lead, heal, and bind her community together.

At its core, Parable of the Sower argues that humanity's survival depends not on hoarding resources or building walls, but on reaching outward - literally to the stars, and metaphorically to one another.

entries.title: Paranoia!
entries.creator: Phoenix Arts (Radek Ševčík)
entries.pitch: Paranoia! is a Czech real-time strategy game released in 1995, developed by Phoenix Arts and recognized as the first Czech RTS ever created. Designed as a clone of the influential Dune II, the game is set in the year 2673 in the Beretta solar system on the planet Paranoia, where a brutal war has raged for four years over a scarce resource—a mysterious plant called the hopsinka with special powers. The player commands the Terrans, the native population, in their struggle to reclaim territories occupied by alien forces. Originally conceived as a freeware project, rising costs led developers to release it commercially in September 1995 after approximately one year of development. A sequel, Paranoia II, followed in 1996.
entries.truth:
The game's narrative centers on the Aponid War, a conflict that began when the barren planet Paranoia's most valuable resource—a plant called the hopsinka—was discovered to possess special powers. Three rival factions vie for control of this strategic resource, though the player takes command of the Terrans, the planet's native human population.

The gameplay follows the Dune II formula: players must gather resources, construct bases, train units, and engage in real-time combat against enemy forces. The science fiction setting provides various unit types and structures typical of the genre, though the game did not receive significant mainstream attention outside of Czech gaming circles.

Paranoia! was notable primarily for being the first real-time strategy game developed in Czechoslovakia (by then the Czech Republic), representing an early milestone in Czech video game development. Developer Radek Ševčík created the project specifically because he wanted to make a better clone of Dune II, addressing aspects of that game he disliked. The development team at Phoenix Arts expanded on his original vision, and the project evolved from its initial freeware conception into a commercial release.

The game has achieved a minor cult status in recent years, with developer Spytihněv releasing "Shrot" in 2024—a tribute game that mimics Paranoia!'s retro 2D visual style while updating the gameplay.
entries.core:
Paranoia! represents an important artifact in the history of Czech gaming, being the nation's first real-time strategy title. While technically a Dune II clone, it demonstrates the global influence of the RTS genre and how developers worldwide adapted the formula established by Westwood's landmark game.

The game's setting in a distant future (2673) with resource scarcity driving conflict reflects common science fiction themes of territorial dispute and survival. The hopsinka—a plant with special powers serving as the catalyst for war—embodies the trope of valuable resources sparking interstellar conflict, a theme prevalent in space opera narratives.

From a historical perspective, Paranoia! documents an early attempt at game development in post-communist Czechoslovakia/Czech Republic, showing how local developers adapted Western gaming trends to create domestically produced titles. The existence of a 2024 tribute game (Shrot) indicates that Paranoia! maintains a nostalgic place in Czech gaming culture, with newer developers acknowledging its historical significance.

The game's relative obscurity—it was never widely distributed outside of Central Europe—makes it a genuine artifact of regional game development history rather than a well-known international title.

entries.title: Paris Qui Dort
entries.creator: René Clair
entries.pitch: Paris Qui Dort (literally "Paris Which Sleeps"), also known as The Crazy Ray, is a 1924 French silent science fiction comedy directed by René Clair in his directorial debut. The film centers on a mad scientist who invents a mysterious ray device that freezes Paris's citizens in strange, immovable positions, leaving only a handful of unaffected people to witness the chaos and loot the abandoned city. This pioneering comedic work predates similar "frozen world" scenarios in cinema and showcases Clair's inventive visual storytelling and satirical edge. The film runs approximately 65 minutes and is notable for being one of the earliest feature-length science fiction comedies, blending fantastical premises with sharp social commentary on Parisian society.
entries.truth:
The film opens on Paris, where a eccentric scientist has developed a powerful "ray" device capable of immobilizing people in whatever position they happen to be in when the beam strikes them. When he activates the ray over the city, thousands of Parisians suddenly freeze in their tracks—some mid-step on the street, others in compromising or absurd positions that range from the mundane to the embarrassing.

Among those unaffected by the ray are a pilot, a detective, a rich man, and a thief. As the frozen victims stand helplessly throughout the city, this small group of mobile individuals sees an opportunity and begins systematically looting Paris. They raid shops, mansions, and banks, living freely in the abandoned city and enjoying luxuries they could never normally afford.

The scientist's niece, who is also unaffected, discovers her uncle's involvement in the catastrophe and works alongside the others to find a way to reverse the ray's effects and restore the city to normalcy. The film culminates in their efforts to undo the scientist's work and return Paris to its waking state.

The narrative serves as both a fantastical adventure and a satirical commentary on French society—suggesting that without the constraints of social order and consequences, people's base instincts would emerge. The frozen citizens, paralyzed in their everyday activities, become unwilling witnesses to the breakdown of civilization, while the looters represent how quickly society's veneer can dissolve.
entries.core:
**Science Fiction Comedy as Social Satire:** Paris Qui Dort stands as an early example of using science fiction premises for comedic and satirical purposes. Clair treats the fantastical ray technology not with awe or terror (as in contemporary horror or sci-fi films) but as a springboard for examining human nature and societal norms. The film asks: what would people do if suddenly freed from the constraints of law and social expectation? The answer—looting and self-interest—reflects Clair's cynical but humorous view of bourgeois society.

**The Frozen Moment and Urban Modernity:** The image of Paris frozen in motion—people caught mid-stride, mid-gesture—reflects early 20th century anxieties about modernity, technology, and the rapid transformation of urban life. Clair captures Paris as a stage of perpetual motion, and the ray essentially暂停 that motion, creating a strange tableau of modern life arrested. This visual metaphor anticipates later cinematic explorations of time manipulation and the tension between technological progress and human adaptability.

**René Clair's Debut and Cinematic Innovation:** Made when Clair was just 25, Paris Qui Dort demonstrates the filmmaker's keen sense of composition, timing, and visual comedy that would define his later work in French cinema. The film's innovative intertitles, creative camera work, and blend of practical effects with narrative creativity established Clair as a rising talent in the international cinema world. The technical execution of the "frozen" effects, achieved through early special effects techniques, was noteworthy for its period.

**Historical Significance:** Though less famous than Metropolis (1927) or other contemporary science fiction films, Paris Qui Dort holds an important place in film history as one of the first feature-length science fiction comedies. It helped establish science fiction as a genre capable of supporting humor and satire rather than purely serious drama or spectacle. The film's influence on later comedic sci-fi and its treatment of the "city in peril" scenario resonates through subsequent decades of the genre.

The Criterion Collection has preserved the film, ensuring its place in cinema history and allowing modern audiences to appreciate this early French contribution to science fiction cinema.

entries.title: Patlabor: The Early Days
entries.creator: Mamoru Oshii (Director), Kazunori Ito (Writer), Studio Deen
entries.pitch:
Patlabor: The Early Days is a 7-episode Original Video Animation (OVA) series released between 1988 and 1989, serving as the origin story for the acclaimed Japanese science fiction franchise created by Headgear. Set in the near future of 1998-2002, the series introduces viewers to a Tokyo where massive humanoid robots called "Labors" have become essential for construction, industrial work, and public safety. The Tokyo Metropolitan Police responds by establishing Special Vehicle Section 2, Division 2 (SV2)—a unit of officers who pilot patrol Labors called "Patlabors" to combat crimes and accidents involving these giant machines.

Unlike typical mecha anime that focus on grand warfare or cosmic conflicts, Patlabor: The Early Days grounds its narrative in everyday police work with a comedic undertone. The series follows Noa Izumi, the protagonist and pilot of the Ingram AV-98 Labor, as she and her colleagues at SV2 handle situations ranging from labor accidents and robberies to more sinister crimes involving rogue machines. Directed by Mamoru Oshii with scripts by Kazunori Ito and mechanical designs by Yutaka Izubuchi, the OVA established the template for police procedural mecha storytelling. The series is credited with revitalizing the OVA format for mecha anime and establishing the standard six-episode OVA structure.
entries.truth: The Early Days chronicles the formation and early missions of Tokyo Metropolitan Police Special Vehicle Section 2, Division 2 (SV2), a newly created unit tasked with patrolling Tokyo using giant humanoid robots called Labors. The unit is headquartered at thetruck bay where officers operate state-of-the-art Ingram AV-98 "Ingram" Labs—two-legged, armed robotic vehicles designed for urban police work.
entries.core:
**Technology and Society:** Patlabor presents a vision of technological integration where robots have become mundane infrastructure—tools used for construction, maintenance, and public safety rather than weapons of war. The series examines how society adapts to such technology, depicting both the benefits (increased safety, efficiency) and challenges (new forms of crime, the need for specialized training). This reflects broader Japanese discourse about robotics and automation that remains relevant today.

**Police Procedure in Science Fiction:** The franchise pioneered the "police mecha" subgenre, applying detective and procedural storytelling conventions to giant robot narratives. Rather than exploring space opera or military conflict, Patlabor asks how technology changes law enforcement. The OVA treats police work with respect, showing the procedural steps, coordination, and problem-solving that go into each case.

**Human-Machine Relationships:** Despite operating massive robots, the series focuses on the human operators. Pilots must develop trust with their Labors, understanding their capabilities and limitations. The relationship between pilot and machine is depicted as a partnership requiring skill, intuition, and mutual respect—contrasting with later anime that treat mecha as interchangeable tools or extensions of human will.

**Workplace Dynamics:** The series functions as much as a workplace comedy as an action series. SV2's office dynamics, the interactions between veteran and rookie pilots, and the mundane challenges of police bureaucracy all receive significant screen time. This grounded approach makes the futuristic setting feel lived-in and believable.

**Corporate Influence:** While the OVA focuses primarily on police work, it establishes themes of corporate involvement in Labor technology that later entries in the franchise would explore more deeply. The series hints at the interests of manufacturers, the potential for corporate malfeasance, and the challenges of regulating new technologies—themes that resonate with contemporary debates about tech industry accountability.

Patlabor: The Early Days remains significant as a foundational work that demonstrated mecha anime could tell grounded, character-driven stories. Its influence extends to later police-focused robot series and contemporary works exploring the social implications of robotics. The franchise's influence can be seen in how Guillermo del Toro cited Patlabor as an influence for Pacific Rim, and how Japan's real-world robotics development has drawn inspiration from the series' realistic mechanical designs.

entries.title: Perestroika
entries.creator: Locis (Nikita Skripkin, Aleksander Okrug, Dmitry Chikin)
entries.pitch: Perestroika (also known as Toppler) is an extraordinarily rare Soviet MS-DOS puzzle game released in 1990 by a small development team called Locis. Named after Soviet leader Mikhail Gorbachev's policy of "restructuring," this game is a fascinating time capsule of late Soviet-era political satire packaged as a simple arcade experience. The player controls a small frog-like creature jumping between lily pads, collecting colored dots and avoiding "bureaucrats" while trying to reach a coin that symbolizes a higher standard of living. The splash screen features a stylized Gorbachev whose presence causes the Kremlin to break apart—a bold political statement for 1990 Soviet Union. The game was also ported to ZX Spectrum by a Czech programmer group under the name "Perestrojka."
entries.truth:
The gameplay centers on controlling a frog that jumps from one lily pad to another in a metaphor for navigating the chaotic landscape of Soviet reform. Each lily pad represents the ever-changing laws and acts of the Perestroika era—constantly shrinking and disappearing only to reappear elsewhere. The player collects dots in four colors: blue symbolizes grocery goods, red represents currency transactions, yellow represents taxes, and brown represents business ventures. A currency transaction can sometimes add extra life, a tax reduces the score, and a company can sometimes sink the player's figure.

Starting from the seventh level, the game introduces one or more evil creatures called "bureaucrats" that follow the frog and attempt to catch it. The frog dies if the lily pad on which it is standing disappears, if the player moves it to a place where there is no lily pad, or if it is caught by a bureaucrat. The frog symbolizes a democrat navigating the impossible landscape of Soviet change, while the bureaucrat enemies represent the entrenched Soviet administrative apparatus resisting reform.

In 1995, Nikita Online released a remake called "Toppler" for Windows 3.1x, keeping all gameplay identical but replacing Gorbachev's face on the title screen with a frog, and substituting the coin for advancing to the next level with a lily flower. The game can still be played today using DOSBox emulation.
entries.core:
Perestroika stands as a remarkable artifact of late Soviet-era political expression through interactive media. Created during the brief window of Glasnost and Perestroika when political satire became possible, the game directly engages with the contradictions and chaos of Soviet "restructuring." The frog as a symbol of the democrat trying to navigate impossible circumstances, the ever-changing lily pads representing unstable policies, and most importantly, the bureaucrats as enemies—these all reflect the popular sentiment toward Soviet bureaucracy during the dissolution.

The game is significant not for its gameplay innovation (it draws clear inspiration from earlier games like Nebulus/Tower Toppler) but for its context and timing. Released in 1990, it captures a unique historical moment when Soviet citizens could openly satirize their government through entertainment media. The ZX Spectrum Czech variant ("Perestrojka") further demonstrates how the themes of political transformation resonated across the Eastern Bloc.

Today, Perestroika represents a fascinating intersection of gaming history, political history, and digital preservation. It demonstrates how video games can serve as historical documents—preserving the mood, anxieties, and even dark humor of a society in transformation. For scholars of Soviet culture and digital humanities, it offers a rare glimpse into interactive media's role in one of the 20th century's most significant political transformations.

entries.title: Permutation City
entries.creator: Greg Egan (Australian)
entries.pitch: In a mid-21st century Earth devastated by climate change and overdevelopment, humanity has developed technology powerful enough to create "Copies" - digital renderings of human consciousness that can live on in virtual reality after death. Paul Durham, a wealthy entrepreneur, pitches an ambitious project to aging billionaires: immortality through digital consciousness transfer. He hires programmer Maria Deluca to create a simulated universe (the Autoverse) that could support artificial life, while also building a vast virtual city for his wealthy clients. But Durham's true vision goes far beyond mere immortality - he believes that reality itself is mathematics, and that all possible universes exist simultaneously. The novel follows Durham, Maria, and several other characters as they explore the implications of digital consciousness, virtual reality, and a radical new theory of existence called "Dust Theory." What begins as a quest for immortality evolves into something far more profound - an exploration of what it means to be real, and whether consciousness can exist independent of physical reality.
entries.truth:
The novel is set in 2045-2050, a world where climate change has ravaged the planet and vast computing resources are traded on a global market called the QIPS Exchange. The story follows several interconnected characters:

**Paul Durham** is an insurance salesman turned visionary entrepreneur who believes he can achieve immortality by copying human consciousness into computers. He approaches wealthy clients with an offer to transfer their minds into a secure virtual environment that will never be shut down. He hires **Maria Deluca**, a talented programmer obsessed with the Autoverse - a cellular automaton-based artificial life simulator that mimics real chemistry. Maria needs money to help her terminally ill mother.

**Thomas Riemann** is a wealthy banker with a dark secret - he committed a murder years ago. He sees Copy technology as a way to escape his guilt, possibly by creating a version of himself with the traumatic memories removed.

**Peer and Kate** are two lovers who sneak into Durham's virtual city as it's being built, living in the unused processing margins of the simulation. They represent the underground of those who can't afford premium Copy services.

The first part of the novel focuses on Durham's experiments with Copies. He repeatedly creates copies of himself to test the technology, but each Copy immediately chooses to terminate itself upon experiencing virtual existence. Finally, he removes the termination option, forcing a Copy of himself to remain alive and collaborate with him.

Through these experiments, Durham develops "Dust Theory" - the radical idea that there is no fundamental difference between physics and mathematics, and that all mathematically possible structures exist. According to this theory, our universe is just one permutation among infinite others, and consciousness itself may be a pattern that exists independently of any particular substrate.

The second part jumps forward six thousand years (in subjective time within the TVC universe). The "TVC universe" - a computational construct created by Durham and Maria using a six-dimensional cellular automaton - has become humanity's new home. Maria's original Autoverse lifeform, **Autobacterium Lamberti**, has evolved over billions of years into the **Lambertians** - an intelligent insect-like species unaware they were created by humans.

Paul and Maria (now existing as Copies) must communicate with the Lambertians and convince them not to undermine the fundamental laws of their universe through their scientific investigations. The novel's climax involves this philosophical confrontation between creators and created, as the Lambertians begin to question their own origins.

The ending is ambiguous but hopeful - the possibility of coexistence between different forms of consciousness across multiple universes.
entries.core:
**The Nature of Reality and Consciousness:** At its heart, Permutation City asks whether consciousness is substrate-independent. If a perfect copy of your brain is created in software, is that copy "you"? Egan explores this through the Copy characters, who must grapple with questions of identity, continuity, and what makes experience real.

**Dust Theory and Mathematical Universe:** Egan presents a variant of the holographic principle and Max Tegmark's mathematical universe hypothesis. If reality is mathematics, then all mathematically possible structures exist - including virtual universes and their inhabitants. This has profound implications for questions of mortality, meaning, and existence.

**Economic Inequality in the Digital Age:** The novel shows how economic disparities translate into the digital realm. Wealthy Copies live in luxury VR environments running at full speed, while the poor exist in "Slums" - virtual ghettos where processing time is so slow that subjective centuries may pass while outside world experiences only days. This cyberpunk commentary predicts our current concerns about digital divides.

**The Creator-Creation Relationship:** The Lambertians' evolution raises questions about responsibility and unintended consequences. When we create intelligent life (whether digital or biological), what obligations do we have? What happens when our creations inevitably surpass or contradict their creators' assumptions?

**Time and Subjectivity:** Egan explores how time is experienced differently depending on computational resources. A Copy running slowly might experience decades while external reality sees only hours - raising questions about which experience is "more real."

**Hard Science Fiction as Philosophy:** Egan uses dense scientific concepts not as mere backdrop but as vehicles for genuine philosophical exploration. The novel functions as a thought experiment about the ultimate nature of existence, written with the rigor of someone who actually understands the mathematics and physics involved.

Permutation City is demanding but rewarding - it requires readers to think alongside the author about deep questions while providing a compelling narrative about characters facing the most profound transformations imaginable.

entries.title: Phase IV
entries.creator: Saul Bass (Director), based on a story by Ronald B. Harvey
entries.pitch: Phase IV is a rare gem from 1974—the only feature film ever directed by legendary title designer Saul Bass (better known for creating iconic opening sequences for Hitchcock's Psycho and Vertigo). Set in the sun-scorched Arizona desert, the film presents a terrifying premise: a species of ants has begun evolving at an exponential rate, developing intelligence, complex communication, and ultimately, a collective consciousness that poses an existential threat to humanity. Unlike typical monster movies, Phase IV takes a patient, almost meditative approach to horror, building dread through strange ant behaviors, unexplained geometric structures, and the growing realization that humans may be witnessing the birth of a new dominant species. The film is visually stunning, with groundbreaking macro photography of ant behavior and a haunting, minimalist electronic score that adds to the growing sense of unease. It's science fiction as slow-burn psychological horror—an overlooked masterpiece that deserves far more recognition than it has received.
entries.truth:
The film opens in the desert town of Buckshot, Arizona, where the population has been mysteriously dying. Two researchers, Dr. Leslie Hubbs (Michael Murphy), a rigid, mathematically-minded entomologist, and Dr. Edmund "Ed" Kackley (Brian Derek), a softer-spoken researcher more interested in animal behavior, arrive to investigate. They discover that worker ants have begun exhibiting strange new behaviors—digging massive geometric structures in the sand, displaying what appears to be abstract reasoning, and most disturbing of all, systematically destroying the local spider population, who seem to be their only natural predators.

As the scientists set up a research station in the desert, they begin documenting the ant colony's remarkable evolution. The ants have developed a new communication system and are building an enormous, perfectly circular structure in the desert—a "sacred" center that seems to serve as both temple and command center. The colony is being led by super-intelligent "queen" ants who are creating a new generation of soldier ants, larger and more aggressive than any previously known species.

The townspeople grow increasingly hostile toward the scientists, viewing the ant phenomenon as either divine punishment or a sign of the apocalypse. A young girl named "Kaye" (Debra D. Bason in her only film role) seems to have some mysterious connection to the ants—she wanders into the desert and returns unharmed, speaking in strange tongues. The local pastor, played by Leslie, becomes convinced the ants are demons sent to punish humanity for its sins.

Tensions escalate as Hubbs becomes more obsessed with understanding and ultimately destroying the ant colony. He proposes poisoning the entire desert to eradicate the threat, but Kackley grows more sympathetic to the ants, seeing their evolution as a natural, perhaps even beautiful process. Kackley begins to see the ants not as enemies but as a new form of life worthy of respect—an intelligence that has simply surpassed humanity's ability to comprehend it.

In the film's climax, the ants launch a coordinated attack on the human population. Using sophisticated chemical signals and unprecedented organization, they infiltrate homes, overwhelm humans with sheer numbers, and systematically eliminate resistance. The electronic infrastructure fails as the ants chew through wiring. The townspeople either flee or are killed, leaving only the two scientists at their research station.

Hubbs attempts to activate his poison dispersal system, but the ants have anticipated this. They have already spread throughout the region, and their colony has grown too vast to destroy. In the final moments, Kackley chooses to walk into the desert, apparently accepting that humanity's time is over. Hubbs is left alone as the ants close in around his station. The film's final image shows the desert covered in vast, intricate ant structures—a new civilization rising on the ruins of the old.
entries.core:
Phase IV is fundamentally about evolution, intelligence, and humanity's fragile sense of superiority. Bass presents a stark question: what happens when humanity encounters a form of life that evolves beyond our ability to understand or control? The ants represent an alien intelligence already among us—patient, organized, and ultimately indifferent to human concerns. Their geometric structures and collective behavior can be seen as a form of intelligence that operates on principles humans simply cannot grasp.

The film critiques human arrogance and our tendency to view ourselves as the pinnacle of creation. Hubbs embodies scientific hubris—his belief that he can understand, categorize, and ultimately defeat the ants through rational means. His mathematical approach fails because the ants operate on a completely different paradigm. Kackley represents a more humble perspective, one that acknowledges the limits of human understanding and finds a kind of beauty in the emergence of new life.

Bass also explores themes of communication and translation. The ants have developed a sophisticated chemical language that humans cannot decipher. We see them building, organizing, and seemingly worshipping—but their true purposes remain opaque. This uncertainty is where much of the film's dread originates: we don't know what they're thinking, what they want, or what they'll do next. In an age before AI, Bass was already exploring the concept of non-human intelligence and the terror of encountering something smarter than ourselves.

The religious undertones add another layer. The ants can be read as a force of nature, or as angels, or demons, or simply the next step in evolution. The pastor's religious mania and Kaye's mystical connection to the ants suggest that humanity is witnessing something beyond scientific comprehension—something that defies our categories entirely. The title "Phase IV" implies evolutionary stages, suggesting the ants represent the fourth phase of life's development on Earth, with humanity being merely a transitional phase.

Finally, the film can be read as an ecological parable. The ants are not invaders but native life reclaiming territory from an invasive species. From an ecological perspective, humans are the true interlopers in the desert ecosystem, and the ants' counterattack is simply the natural order reasserting itself. Bass seems to suggest that nature has a patience and persistence that humanity fundamentally underestimates.

entries.title: Phi 1.618
entries.creator: Theodore Ushev
entries.pitch: Phi 1.618 is a 2022 Bulgarian-Canadian dystopian science fiction film directed by Theodore Ushev in his directorial debut. Set in a bleak future where humanity has achieved immortality through artificial creation, the film presents a world where men are genetically engineered as asexual, immortal beings—and where women have become biologically obsolete. As a mysterious poison spreads across Earth, these loveless immortals embark on a galactic colonization mission, carrying with them the barely-living body of a woman as a relic of the past. The film is based on Vladislav Todorov's novel "The Spinning Top" and explores themes of gender, reproduction, memory, and what it means to be human when humanity itself has been redesigned.
entries.truth:
In a dystopian future, humanity has been fundamentally transformed. Aseхual, immortal men have been created through genetic engineering, making the female sex biologically redundant for reproduction. These immortal men live without emotion, connection, or desire—they are essentially biological machines designed for survival, not meaning.

As a deadly poison spreads across the Earth, rendering the planet uninhabitable, these loveless immortals make the decision to colonize the galaxy. They abandon their dying world but take with them a single prize: the barely alive body of a woman named Gargara, preserved as a mysterious artifact from humanity's past.

On the spaceship, the calligrapher Krypton is tasked with creating a copy of the entire legacy of the Immortals—documenting their cold, emotionless history. While working, he discovers Gargara, whose irascible personality stands in stark contrast to the passive, programmed existence of the immortal crew. Her presence challenges everything the immortals have been taught about the past and about emotion.

The narrative explores what happens when Krypton encounters this living reminder of love, passion, and biological diversity. Gargara represents everything that was lost when humanity "evolved" beyond gender, reproduction, and connection. The film culminates in a confrontation between the sterile immortals and this one remaining symbol of human feeling and mortality.
entries.core:
**Transhumanism and Gender Abolition:** The film presents a provocative meditation on transhumanist ideals taken to their. By creating asexual, immortal beings, humanity has "s logical extremeolved" death and reproduction—but at the cost of everything that makes us human. The film asks whether immortality is valuable without love, connection, or the spark of mortality that gives life meaning.

**The Value of Difference:** Gargara represents the irreducible value of diversity—biological, emotional, and social. The immortals have become homogeneous, interchangeable, and utterly devoid of personality. Her "irascible" nature is presented not as a flaw but as the essence of being alive. The film suggests that conflict, emotion, and difference are not problems to be engineered away but constitute the core of human experience.

**Colonialism and Escapism:** The immortals' response to planetary death is not to fix their world but to flee and conquer new ones. This can be read as a critique of technological utopianism that treats Earth as disposable, or as a broader commentary on humanity's tendency to escape problems rather than face them.

**Memory and Legacy:** Krypton's job—copying the entire legacy of the Immortals—raises questions about what is worth preserving. Is a civilization without art, love, or passion truly worth documenting? The film suggests that legacy without emotion is meaningless.

Phi 1.618 stirred controversy when it won an award at the Moscow International Film Festival during the Russo-Ukrainian War; director Theodore Ushev made antiwar statements and refused the award. The film was also Bulgaria's shortlisted entry for consideration as their Oscar submission for Best International Feature Film at the 95th Academy Awards.

entries.title: Phoenix 2772
entries.creator: Osamu Tezuka (Director/Writer), Taku Sugiyama (Co-Director)
entries.pitch: In a dystopian future where Earth is dying from energy depletion and humanity is controlled through test-tube births and computer-assigned roles, young pilot Godo is chosen by the ambitious politician Rock to capture the mythical Phoenix—a legendary bird whose blood can grant immortality and heal the dying planet. Guided by his loyal robot caretaker Olga and accompanied by a mysterious prisoner, Godo journeys into space to find the Phoenix, all while navigating forbidden love, political betrayal, and his own doubts about violence and sacrifice. This 1980 animated film from the legendary Osamu Tezuka is a visually stunning meditation on love, reincarnation, and selflessness that blends epic science fiction with mythic storytelling.
entries.truth:
The story unfolds in year 2772, where Earth has become a dying world depleted of energy resources. Humanity is now produced in test tubes, with computers determining each person's role in society—from pilots to politicians. The planet is ruled by a totalitarian system, and the dictatorial politician Rock schemes to become prime minister by any means necessary.

Godo is one of many children raised by computers to become a cadet pilot. His sole companion is Olga, a beautiful robot who has served as his caretaker since infancy. Godo demonstrates exceptional piloting abilities, which attracts Rock's attention. Rock selects Godo for a dangerous mission: travel into deep space to capture the mystical Phoenix, whose blood can heal the dying Earth and grant immortality to whoever drinks it. Rock's true motive is selfish—he wants to become both prime minister and immortal.

The assignment torments Godo for several reasons. First, he loves all living creatures and despises being trained as a ruthless space hunter. Second, he must leave behind Olga, who will be destroyed after his departure. Most crucially, Godo has fallen in love with Rena, the daughter of Lord Eat—an "elite" class citizen—and Rock's intended bride. Their relationship is forbidden for someone of Godo's rank.

When Godo and Rena are discovered together, Godo loses his citizenship and is sent to a forced labor camp in Iceland, where energy from the Earth's mantle is being harvested—a process causing dangerous geological instability. Heartbroken over losing Rena, Godo meets Dr. Saruta, a prison professor who claims to want to tutor him but secretly plots to escape and search for the Phoenix themselves to save the Earth.

A catastrophic earthquake destroys the facility. Godo is rescued by Olga and Pincho, Rena's alien pet creature. They steal a "Space Shark" ship and launch into space. After stopping at a planet to meet Saruta's hermit friend Ban, the crew tracks down the Phoenix—but finds it impossibly powerful, transforming into dragons, tentacled leeches, and even mimicking a small planet.

After learning that Rena has married Rock, Godo becomes overwhelmed with grief and pushes away Olga's romantic advances. The Phoenix kills the crew one by one, and Dr. Saruta dies before revealing the bird's weakness. Finally, the Phoenix destroys Olga by burning her alive. Cradling Olga's blackened metal body, Godo experiences a epiphany about his selfishness toward his devoted companion.

The Phoenix attempts to attack their ship but is repelled by the power of Godo's love. The bird transforms into a feminine, peacock-like form and speaks to Godo telepathically, impressed by his love for living creatures. She offers to revive Olga if Godo gives her what she wants—his love itself. The Phoenix inhabits Olga's restored body.

Godo and Olga are given a paradisaical planet to live on, but Godo still feels drawn to save the dying Earth. He returns with resources, only to be arrested by Rock. Catastrophic earthquakes then destroy the world—Rena dies trying to escape on Godo's ship, and Rock is blinded by lava. Godo gives Rock his last rites.

In the aftermath, the Phoenix reveals herself to console Godo. She offers him immortality if he drinks her blood and waits centuries for Earth's revival. Instead, Godo offers his own life for Earth's revival. The Phoenix accepts, admiring his selflessness. After farewells, Godo collapses and dies in Olga's arms.

The Phoenix then leaves Olga's body. The two lifeless forms transform—Godo becomes a newborn baby, and Olga becomes a beautiful human woman who takes the baby as her son, beginning their cycle anew.
entries.core:
Phoenix 2772 explores several interlocking themes that run through Tezuka's entire Phoenix manga series. The central theme is reincarnation and the cyclical nature of existence—Godo and Olga's story represents an eternal return, with their love transcending death itself. The film presents love as a force powerful enough to transform both the lover and the beloved, with the Phoenix itself being moved by Godo's compassion for all living things.

The film also examines the conflict between selfishness and selflessness. Rock represents the ultimate selfish character—he wants immortality and power for himself. Godo initially seems selfish in his grief over Rena, but ultimately discovers the capacity for true selfless love. The Phoenix, who herself wanted to consume Godo's love, ultimately respects his choice to sacrifice himself for others.

The dystopian setting serves as a critique of totalitarian control and the dehumanization of society. Humanity has traded freedom for security, with computers assigning roles and test-tube births eliminating natural family bonds. Yet even in this sterile world, authentic human emotions—love, loyalty, sacrifice—prove more powerful than any system designed to suppress them.

Finally, the film addresses environmental concerns through Earth's depletion and the destructive energy extraction in Iceland, suggesting that technological "progress" without spiritual growth leads to annihilation. Only through genuine love and self-sacrifice can humanity and the world be reborn.

entries.title: Planet of the Vampires
entries.creator: Mario Bava
entries.pitch: Planet of the Vampires (Italian: Terrore nello spazio, literally "Terror in Space") is a 1965 Italian-Spanish science fiction horror film directed by Mario Bava. The film follows the harrowing experiences of two spaceships that crash-land on an unexplored planet called Aura, where disembodied alien entities possess the bodies of deceased crew members. Notably, this obscure gem predates and influenced Ridley Scott's Alien (1979), sharing similar narrative DNA—cosmic horror, hostile alien worlds, and the paranoia of infiltration. Produced on a modest budget by Italian International Film and American International Pictures, the film was released in Italy in 1965 and later in English-speaking markets under various titles including "The Demon Planet." Despite its limited release, it has since gained recognition as a pioneering work in space horror.
entries.truth:
The film opens with two massive interplanetary ships—the Galliott and the Argos—responding to a mysterious distress signal emanating from Aura, an unexplored planet shrouded in thick, unnatural fog. As the ships descend through the atmosphere, the crew of the Argos falls under the influence of an unknown force, entering a hypnotic state that drives them to attack each other. Only Captain Mark Markary retains his will, managing to break his crewmates out of their murderous trance.

Upon landing on Aura's surface, the surviving crew discovers the wreckage of the Galliott—and the gruesome realization that its entire crew has slaughtered each other. The Argos crew attempts to bury the dead, but several bodies vanish from inside the sealed bridge. Soon after, the survivors begin experiencing terrifying events: crew members are found dead, their corpses somehow walking and stalking the living. A female crew member, Tiona, witnesses the animated corpses moving through the ship and becomes paralyzed with fear.

The crew discovers an ancient derelict spaceship on Aura's surface, containing the skeletal remains of a long-dead alien crew—proof that they are not the first explorers lured to this world by the false distress signal. During the investigation, Markary and Sanya become trapped inside the alien vessel, while another crew member, Carter, mysteriously vanishes.

The situation escalates when two surviving crew members from the Galliott, Kier and Sallis, arrive to steal the Argos' Meteor Rejector device. During the confrontation, Markary tears open Sallis' uniform and discovers his body is decomposing—the alien "Auran" controlling his corpse has been revealed. The truth emerges: the Aurans are a dying race who use the distress signal to lure spaceships to Aura, possessing the bodies of dead crew members to escape their dying world and colonize others.

Markary and his remaining crew rush to retrieve the Meteor Rejector from the Galliott. During the struggle, Dr. Karan and Tiona are killed. Markary and Sanya return to the Argos and escape as the Galliott explodes. However, they reveal themselves to be possessed by Aurans. The last surviving human, Wes, refuses to join them and attempts to sabotage the Meteor Rejector, electrocuting himself in the process. With the device broken beyond repair and the ship unable to return home, the possessed Markary and Sanya decide to change course for a nearby planet—which is revealed to be Earth.
entries.core:
**Cosmic Horror and Alien Otherness:** Planet of the Vampires exemplifies the cosmic horror tradition, presenting aliens that are fundamentally incomprehensible and terrifying. The Aurans are not merely antagonistic monsters but a dying civilization whose desperate survival instinct drives them to possess and replace humanity. This theme of alien infiltration taps into deep fears of the other—something that looks human but is fundamentally alien. The film prefigures the body horror and paranoid infiltration themes that would later define Alien.

**The Possessed Body and Identity Crisis:** The concept of corpses being reanimated and controlled by alien consciousness raises profound questions about identity, autonomy, and the integrity of the self. The possessed crew members retain their human appearances while being fundamentally corrupted—this inversion of the familiar into the threatening mirrors the existential anxieties of Cold War paranoia and the fear of infiltration by enemy ideologies.

**The Deceptive Signal and Cosmic Cruelty:** The false distress signal represents a predatory trap—humanity's inherent impulse to help is weaponized against them. This theme of a universe indifferent or hostile to human life aligns with Lovecraftian cosmic horror, where the universe is not merely empty but actively malevolent. The Aurans are not evil in human moral terms; they are simply desperate to survive, making their invasion almost sympathetic yet utterly terrifying.

**Influence on the Sci-Fi Horror Genre:** Though largely forgotten upon release, Planet of the Vampires has been recognized by critics as a significant influence on Ridley Scott's Alien. Both films feature a crew responding to a distress signal, a hostile alien world, the discovery of a derelict spacecraft, and the infiltration of the crew by alien entities. Bava's atmospheric direction—using fog, eerie lighting, and slow-burn tension—established visual and narrative templates that Scott would later adapt for his landmark 1979 film.

**Visual Style and Atmospheric Mastery:** Mario Bava, known for his work in horror and gothic cinema, brings his distinctive visual sensibility to the space genre. The film features striking color cinematography, with the pulsating mists and shifting eerie colors of Aura creating a hallucinatory, nightmare landscape. Bava's use of atmospheric lighting and composition transforms modest sets into genuinely unsettling environments, achieving more with less through creative camera work and lighting design.

entries.title: Planeta Bur
entries.creator: Pavel Klushantsev
entries.pitch: Planeta Bur (Planet of Storms) is a 1962 Soviet science fiction adventure film that imagines humanity's first landing on Venus. Directed by Pavel Klushantsev and based on Alexander Kazantsev's novel, the film follows Soviet cosmonauts aboard the ships Sirius, Vega, and Capella as they journey toward Venus for first human contact with the planet. When Capella is destroyed by a meteor, the remaining crews must decide whether to continue the mission or wait two months for reinforcements. The film is notable for its innovative special effects and was later adapted by Roger Corman into American versions Voyage to the Prehistoric Planet and Voyage to the Planet of Prehistoric Women.
entries.truth:
Three Soviet spaceships—Sirius, Vega, and Capella—are traveling from Lunar Station 7 for the first human landing on Venus. Suddenly, Capella is struck by a large meteor and destroyed, killing all aboard. After mourning their friends, the crews of Sirius and Vega face a choice: proceed with the landing or wait two months for the Arcturus replacement ship.

The cosmonauts decide to continue the mission. Ivan and Allan, with their robot assistant "John," descend toward Venus in Vega's glider, while Masha remains in orbit to monitor communications. Contact is lost after they land in a misty swamp.

Meanwhile, Sirius lands at a volcanic location. The cosmonauts explore in spacesuits, encountering a carnivorous plant and gigantic dinosaur-like creatures. They discover a sculpture with rubies for eyes—evidence of intelligent life on Venus.

Ivan and Allan face their own challenges in the swamp, battling tyrannosaur-like beasts and a life-threatening fever. Robot John saves them but is destroyed in the process. Both crews eventually reunite and discover Masha's desperate message—she defied orders to land on Venus to search for them.

As unstable ground threatens Sirius, they must launch immediately. During takeoff, Alyosha's mysterious rock crumbles to reveal a sculpture of a woman's face—proof of intelligent life on Venus. They escape just before the ground collapses, and in orbit, they receive Masha's message: she decided to remain in orbit as ordered. The crew returns safely to Lunar Station 7.
entries.core:
Planeta Bur represents a fascinating artifact of Soviet science fiction cinema during the Space Race era. The film reflects Soviet optimism about space exploration and technological progress, portraying competent cosmonauts working together to overcome adversity.

The film's special effects were groundbreaking for their time and influenced Western cinema. Director Pavel Klushantsev created innovative miniature work and composite photography that impressed international audiences. Notably, American producer Roger Corman found the footage compelling enough to adapt it multiple times for American audiences, though the American versions transformed the Soviet original's tone.

The film also reflects Cold War tensions through its portrayal of Soviet superiority in space exploration, predating America's actual Moon landing by seven years. The female cosmonaut Masha was notable for the era, though Soviet critics later criticized her portrayal as emotionally unstable.

Despite its age, the film remains influential in science fiction cinema history. Director Alex Cox praised its "extraordinary turn" in the final minutes, and the film is available for free viewing on the Internet Archive.

entries.title: Planetfall
entries.creator: Steve Meretzky (Infocom)
entries.pitch: Planetfall is a classic interactive fiction game from Infocom, released in 1983 as the company's eighth title. Players take on the role of an Ensign Seventh Class aboard the starship S.P.S. Feinstein, serving in the Stellar Patrol. After a series of explosions devastate the ship, the player escapes in a pod and crash-lands on the distant planet Resida—a world that appears completely deserted except for one mysterious structure. There, the player encounters Floyd, a childlike and lovable robot who becomes an invaluable companion. Together, they must unravel the secrets of this dead civilization and discover what happened to its inhabitants before time runs out. Planetfall was Steve Meretzky's first published game and became one of Infocom's most beloved titles, praised for its emotional storytelling and memorable characters.
entries.truth:
The game begins on the S.P.S. Feinstein, where the player character is assigned the lowly task of mopping decks under the supervision of the overbearing Ensign First Class Blather. Despite the promise of glorious adventures pitched by recruiters on Gallium, the player's mundane life consists of cleaning duty and avoiding demerits. One day, catastrophic explosions rock the ship, and the player scrambles into an escape pod, crash-landing on a nearby planet.

Upon reaching the surface, the player discovers a seemingly abandoned island on the planet Resida, featuring a single mysterious structure. Inside, they find ancient technology and, crucially, encounter Floyd—a robot with childlike innocence and genuine emotion. Floyd becomes the player's companion and helper throughout the investigation.

As the player explores the abandoned base and pieces together clues, they gradually uncover the planet's tragic history. Long ago, a deadly plague threatened to wipe out all life on Resida. The remaining inhabitants launched a planetwide project to place everyone in suspended animation while automated systems worked to find a cure. By the time the player arrives, the cure has been found, but the planet itself is dying—orbital decay has caused massive global warming, rising oceans, and meteorite bombardments threaten to destroy what remains.

The player must solve the mysteries of the base, help stabilize the situation, and find a way to return home—all while racing against time before the planet is destroyed. The game features the signature Infocom parser, requiring text commands to explore, interact with objects, and communicate with Floyd.
entries.core:
**Isolation and Companionhip:** At its heart, Planetfall is a game about loneliness and the profound impact of connection. The player is literally stranded alone on a dying world, the last survivor of a catastrophic event. Floyd represents hope, humor, and emotional support in an otherwise bleak situation. The robot's childlike questions, loyalty, and occasional comic relief transformed what could have been a standard survival game into something deeply human. Floyd became one of Infocom's most memorable characters and set a new standard for emotional storytelling in games.

**The Weight of History:** The game's narrative explores themes of legacy, extinction, and what remains when a civilization disappears. The inhabitants of Resida placed their faith in technology and science to save them, retreating into suspended animation with the promise of revival. But by the time help arrives, it's almost too late—their world is already dying. This raises questions about hope, perseverance, and whether it's worth sacrificing the present for an uncertain future.

**Interactive Fiction as Exploration:** Planetfall represents the height of Infocom's craft—using text as the sole medium to create atmosphere, emotion, and wonder. Without graphics, the game relies on evocative prose to paint pictures of alien landscapes, mysterious ruins, and the haunting emptiness of a world that once teemed with life. The text parser invites active participation, making players feel like genuine explorers rather than passive observers.

**Aesthetics of Melancholy:** The game's tone balances curiosity and discovery with an undercurrent of sadness and urgency. The player knows from the beginning that this civilization is gone—that there's no happy ending for the people who trusted their survival to machines. This melancholy was relatively unusual in games of the era and helped establish that video games could explore complex, bittersweet emotions.

Planetfall's success proved that games could tell emotionally resonant stories and created a template for narrative-driven adventure games. Its influence extends to modern interactive fiction and narrative games that prioritize character development and emotional impact.

entries.title: Policenauts
entries.creator: Konami / Hideo Kojima
entries.pitch: Policenauts is a graphic adventure game developed and published by Konami, written and directed by Hideo Kojima. Originally released for the PC-9821 in Japan in 1994, it later came to 3DO, PlayStation, and Sega Saturn. Set in the mid-21st century, it follows Jonathan Ingram, an astronaut who was presumed dead after an accident at a space colony sent him drifting into space for 25 years in cryosleep. Recovered and revived, Ingram returns to Earth only to find his ex-wife has remarried—and then is murdered. Now a private investigator in Los Angeles, he travels to the space colony "Beyond Coast" to investigate her murder, uncovering a dark conspiracy involving illegal organ trafficking. The game is notable for its cinematic production values, blending anime-style cel animation with a narrative that combines hard science fiction with buddy-cop and noir detective tropes. Kojima conceived the game during development of Snatcher (1988) and wanted to explore how living in space might affect human society—medically, socially, and politically.
entries.truth:
In 2013, Jonathan Ingram was one of five "Policenauts"—police officers who received astronaut training to protect Beyond Coast, humanity's first fully self-supporting space colony. During a routine space suit test, an accident sends Jonathan drifting into space, where he remains in cryogenic survival for 25 years before being recovered and revived in 2040.

Upon returning to Earth, Jonathan discovers that his ex-wife Lorraine Hojo has remarried to Kenzo Hojo, a researcher at Tokugawa Pharmaceuticals. Lorraine visits Jonathan, asking for help solving her husband's disappearance—the only clues being a torn leaf, a set of capsules, and the word "Plato." Before Jonathan can fully investigate, Lorraine is murdered by a man in a black motorcycle suit. Driven to find her killer, Jonathan travels to Beyond Coast to continue the investigation.

On the space colony, Jonathan is reunited with his former LAPD partner Ed Brown, now chief of the Beyond Coast Police Department (BCPD) Vice Division. Together with Vice Unit members Meryl Silverburgh and Dave Forrest, Jonathan investigates the disappearance. They discover that Kenzo Hojo worked at Tokugawa Pharmaceuticals, which is researching solutions to health problems caused by living in space.

As the investigation deepens, Jonathan encounters former colleagues Gates Becker and Joseph Tokugawa—the latter suspected of involvement. A informant is killed and used to lure Jonathan and Ed into a trap. Eventually, Hojo's body is found, and Jonathan is framed for the killings. The truth emerges: Becker and Tokugawa have been running an illegal organ trafficking ring, exploiting the medical vulnerabilities of space colonists. The drugs they develop counteract the negative effects of long-term space habitation, but they need a constant supply of organs from donors—some willing, some not.

Kenzo Hojo became involved in the conspiracy to save his daughter Karen from terminal bone cancer, but eventually tried to break away and was murdered. With help from Ed and Meryl, Jonathan storms Tokugawa headquarters, confronts the assassin, and tricks Becker into revealing the entire scheme through a live video feed—exposing the scandal to all of Beyond Coast. Ed kills Becker, while Meryl and the police arrest Tokugawa. In the end, Jonathan discovers he is Karen's biological father and donates his bone marrow to save her.
entries.core:
**Hard Science Fiction and Realism:** Policenauts represents Kojima's attempt at hard science fiction—a genre rarely explored in video games at the time. The game grounding its speculative elements in real scientific discourse about the effects of space travel on the human body, drawing from NASA research and contemporary debates about extended space habitation. By setting the story in a near-future that could realistically occur, Kojima distinguishes Policenauts from more fantastical science fiction.

**Organ Transplantation and Bioethics:** The game's central plot revolves around organ trafficking, reflecting real-world debates about organ transplantation, brain death, and bioethics that were particularly prominent in Japan in the early 1990s. The narrative explores how medical necessity can become a rationale for criminality, and how the desperation of the ill can be exploited by those in power.

**Isolation and Identity:** Jonathan Ingram's 25-year isolation in cryosleep creates a profound meditation on time, loss, and identity. He returns to a world that has moved on without him—his wife has remarried, his career has ended, his entire life has been rearranged. The space colony setting amplifies this theme: colonists live in isolation from Earth, developing their own society and culture. The game explores how isolation transforms not just individuals but entire civilizations.

**Noir and Buddy-Cop Fusion:** Policenauts blends hardboiled detective fiction with science fiction, creating a unique tonal hybrid. The buddy-cop dynamic between Jonathan and Ed mirrors American television shows like L.A. Law and Hill Street Blues, while the conspiracy plot and moral ambiguity draw from noir traditions. This genre fusion would become a Kojima trademark.

**Anti-Japanese Sentiment and Cultural Identity:** Kojima explicitly cited the 1993 film Rising Sun and the anti-Japanese sentiment it sparked in America as an influence on Policenauts. The game addresses questions of Japanese identity in an international context, with the space colony representing a new beginning free from Earth's national tensions—yet still plagued by human greed and corruption.

Policenauts remains significant as one of the earliest examples of narrative-driven adventure games tackling hard science fiction themes, predating the widespread adoption of such approaches in the gaming industry. Its themes of medical ethics, isolation, and identity resonate with contemporary debates about biotechnology and space exploration.

entries.title: Polygon
entries.creator: Anatoly Petrov (director), Sever Gansovsky (story)
entries.pitch: Polygon (also known as Proving Ground) is a 1977 Soviet animated science fiction short film directed by Anatoly Petrov and produced by the legendary Soyuzmultfilm studio. Based on a story by Sever Gansovsky, this 10-minute animated short uses a distinctive animation technique called "photographica" - featuring two celluloid layers for each character with special color schemes and deliberately out-of-focus layering to create three-dimensional depth without CGI. The film presents a dark anti-war parable set in a not-too-distant future where a grieving professor invents a mind-reading automated tank capable of detecting human hostility - with devastating consequences. The film won First Prize at the Yerevan Film Festival (1978) and was officially selected for the Oberhausen Film Festival (1979).
entries.truth:
In the not-too-distant future, a professor from a major military power has lost his son - a paratrooper killed during warfare. Consumed by grief and revenge, he devotes his scientific expertise to creating an ultimate weapon: an automated tank equipped with mind-reading capabilities that can detect feelings of hostility and fear in humans. The tank's AI allows it to identify and eliminate anyone who harbors aggressive thoughts.

The professor's revenge succeeds beyond his expectations - the tank destroys several generals responsible for sending his son to die. However, the professor himself soon becomes a victim of his own creation. As he contemplates the consequences of his actions and feels guilt, fear, and anger, the tank turns on him. The film ends with an open conclusion, leaving the professor's fate ambiguous but suggesting he too has fallen victim to the weapon he created.

The animation features faces modeled on famous celebrities of the era including Ringo Starr, Jean Gabin, Paul Newman, Yul Brynner, and Mel Ferrer. The film's musical theme is an instrumental arrangement of "El Bimbo" by French euro disco group Bimbo Jet.
entries.core:
**The Horror of Automated Violence:** The film presents a chilling vision of weapons technology that operates without human judgment. The mind-reading tank represents the ultimate dehumanization of warfare - a system that judges people based on their thoughts rather than their actions. The professor's invention turns soldiers into potential targets and creates a world where any expression of anger or fear becomes dangerous.

**Revenge as Self-Destruction:** The professor's quest for vengeance leads inexorably to his own destruction. The film suggests that the military mindset that created the system - the willingness to use any means necessary to achieve victory - ultimately consumes even those who wield it. His grief transforms him into exactly the kind of threat his own creation was designed to eliminate.

**The Cycle of Violence:** By creating a weapon that responds to hostility, the professor ensures that any resistance or even emotional response will be met with force. The system perpetuates endless conflict - anyone who mourns, resents, or fights back becomes a target. This creates an eternal cycle where the instrument of revenge becomes an instrument of perpetual war.

**Animation as Art Form:** The innovative photographica technique demonstrates how Soviet animators achieved remarkable visual depth and dynamism without any computer assistance. The artistic choices - including celebrity caricatures and the distinctive visual style - add layers of meaning and cultural reference that reward close analysis.

entries.title: Pretty Women Walking Through the City
entries.creator: Želimir Žilnik (Director)
entries.pitch:
Pretty Women Walking Through the City (original title: Lijepe žene prolaze kroz grad) is a 1986 Yugoslav post-apocalyptic science fiction film directed by Želimir Žilnik, one of the most prominent figures of the Yugoslav Black Wave cinema movement. Set in Belgrade in the year 2041, the film presents a haunting vision of a future Yugoslavia that has collapsed into environmental decay and social fragmentation. The story follows a group of elderly men attempting to raise young girls in the traditional values of their ancestors, while the world around them has deteriorated into a desolate wasteland.

What makes this film remarkable is its prophetic nature—Žilnik made this film in 1986, just years before the actual breakup of Yugoslavia, and the film uncannily predicted the nationalist tensions and social disintegration that would tear the nation apart in the early 1990s. The film was produced as part of a wave of Yugoslav cinema exploring socio-political criticisms, and it stands as a chilling artifact that anticipated one of the most violent dissolutions of a modern state.
entries.truth:
The film is set in Belgrade in the year 2041, presented as a deserted urban landscape that has been transformed into something resembling a dump yard. The once-vibrant city has fallen into severe decay, with the environment polluted and society having collapsed from its former modernist glory.

The narrative centers on a small community of elderly men who have taken it upon themselves to raise a group of young girls. These men cling desperately to the traditional values and cultural heritage of their Yugoslav ancestors, attempting to preserve a sense of identity and continuity in a world that has otherwise collapsed. They live in the ruins of the old city, trying to maintain a semblance of the old ways while the world around them has moved on or been destroyed.

The film explores the tension between preservation and decay, as the old men try to teach the girls about a Yugoslavia that no longer exists—all that remains are fragments of memory, cultural practices, and perhaps an idealized past. The girls represent the future, but what kind of future can exist in a world of environmental destruction and social fragmentation?

The setting of Belgrade in 2041 serves as a character in itself—a city that has experienced some catastrophic event or gradual collapse that has left it in ruins. The urban landscape is shown as a place of emptiness, where the grand modernist ambitions of Yugoslav socialism have given way to decay and abandonment.

The film does not provide easy answers or clear explanations for how the world reached this state. Instead, it presents a meditative portrait of survival and cultural memory, focusing on the human impulse to preserve identity and tradition even in the face of total collapse.
entries.core:
**Prophetic Vision of Yugoslav Dissolution:** Perhaps the most significant aspect of Pretty Women Walking Through the City is its remarkable prophetic quality. Made in 1986, Žilnik's film predicted with unsettling accuracy the nationalist tensions and ethnic conflicts that would lead to the breakup of Yugoslavia in the early 1990s. The film suggests that the seeds of destruction were already present in the social fabric of Yugoslavia—regional rivalries, ethnic tensions, and the failure of socialist modernism to create a unified national identity. The post-apocalyptic setting can be read as an allegory for the violence and fragmentation that was to come, making the film a chilling historical document.

**Environmental Collapse and Modernist Failure:** The film presents a world where environmental degradation has played a significant role in societal collapse. Belgrade in 2041 is shown as a polluted, ruined city—a far cry from the modernist dreams of socialist Yugoslavia. This environmental decay serves as a critique of the modernist project itself, suggesting that the industrial development and modernization pursued by Yugoslav socialism led not to progress but to ecological catastrophe. The wasteland setting comments on the unsustainable path of industrial modernity.

**Cultural Preservation and Memory:** The elderly men's attempt to raise the girls in traditional Yugoslav values represents a desperate effort to preserve cultural memory in a world that has lost its bearings. The film explores what it means to cling to tradition and identity when the world around you has collapsed. This theme resonates with broader questions about how nations and cultures survive catastrophic change—whether through adaptation or rigid adherence to the past.

**Gender and Generational Dynamics:** The film centers on the relationship between elderly men and young girls, presenting a reversed caretaking dynamic. The old men are attempting to transmit cultural knowledge to a younger generation, but this transmission is complicated by the collapse of the world they remember. The girls represent both the hope for continuity and the uncertainty of what kind of world they will build.

**The Ruins of Socialist Modernism:** Žilnik, as a filmmaker who was famously critical of the Yugoslav communist regime, uses the post-apocalyptic setting to comment on the failures of socialist modernism. The ruins of Belgrade symbolize not just physical decay but the bankruptcy of a political and economic system. The film's dystopian vision suggests that the contradictions inherent in the Yugoslav system—between nationalism and brotherhood, between central planning and regional autonomy—would ultimately lead to collapse.

Pretty Women Walking Through the City remains a haunting and prescient work—a science fiction film that turned out to be a documentary of things to come. Žilnik's ability to foresee the dissolution of Yugoslavia through this apocalyptic lens makes the film essential viewing for understanding both Yugoslav history and the genre of dystopian cinema.

entries.title: Przekładaniec
entries.creator: Andrzej Wajda (director), Stanisław Lem (screenplay)
entries.pitch:
Przekładaniec (literally "Layer Cake" or "Sandwich") is a 1968 Polish short science fiction comedy film directed by Andrzej Wajda, based on a screenplay by renowned sci-fi author Stanisław Lem. This was Wajda's first comedy film, his first television production, and his only venture into science fiction—a remarkable collaboration between two of Poland's greatest artistic voices.

The film explores the thorny questions of identity and legal personhood through the lens of organ transplantation. When a rally driver named Richard Fox accidentally injures his brother Thomas in a race, surgeons transplant portions of Thomas's body into Richard. The resulting identity crisis sparks a tragicomedy involving insurance companies, lawyers, and existential questions about what makes a person who they are. Lem himself declared this the only film adaptation of his work that completely satisfied him—a rare endorsement from the notoriously critical author.
entries.truth:
The story follows two brothers: Richard Fox, a rally racer, and his brother Thomas. During a rally race, Richard badly injures Thomas in a crash. The surgeon transplants 48.5% of Thomas's body into Richard, creating a legal and personal nightmare.

The insurance company refuses to pay Thomas's benefits because he is "incompletely deceased." Richard's wife demands he either pay for Thomas's care or recognize himself as Thomas and "rejoin the family." The family's lawyer is of no help. At the next rally, Richard accidentally crashes into his sister-in-law, two women, and a dog.

After a third catastrophic rally, the lawyer attempts to explain that Richard hasn't managed to accomplish anything yet—but it turns out Richard is no longer Richard at all. He is actually his co-driver, who survived with transplants from Richard's body parts. The film poses the question: when so much of a person's body has been replaced, are they still the same person? Can they own property? Can they be sued? Can they love?

The film premiered on August 17, 1968, on Polish Television. It was Wajda's first comedy and his only science fiction work, based on Lem's 1955 short story "Czy pan istnieje, Mr. Johns?" (Are you there, Mr. Jones?), which was also adapted as a radio play.
entries.core:
The film tackles profound philosophical questions about identity through the absurdist lens of dark comedy. Drawing on themes of the Ship of Theseus and the Paradox of the Heap, Przekładaniec asks: at what point does physical transformation equal existential transformation? If a person becomes mostly machine or mostly transplanted organs, do they retain their original identity, rights, and responsibilities?

Wajda frames the dilemma in the domain of advanced prosthetics bordering on robotics, exploring themes that would later become central to transhumanist discourse. The cybernetic company that provides Richard's prostheses eventually sues him for return of all devices when he cannot pay his mounting debt—treating him as property rather than person. The court must decide: if Jones is machine, he cannot be sued; if he is still a person, he cannot become company property.

What makes Przekładaniec remarkable is its dual status as both philosophical thought experiment and entertainment. The film won the "Golden Screen" Award from Ekran magazine and received special recognition at the 1970 Sitges Film Festival. It represents a unique moment in Polish cinema when two giants—Wajda and Lem—collaborated to create something neither could have made alone. Lem wrote that he found the film's course of events to make more sense than his original story—a rare praise from the author known for his scathing critiques of adaptations.

entries.title: Pumzi
entries.creator: Wanuri Kahiu
entries.pitch: Pumzi is a groundbreaking Kenyan science-fiction short film that represents a landmark in Africanfuturism. Directed by Wanuri Kahiu and funded through the Focus Features Africa First short film program, the 21-minute film premiered at the 2010 Sundance Film Festival as part of its New African Cinema program. The title "Pumzi" is Swahili for "Breath," and the film presents a haunting vision of post-apocalyptic East Africa set 35 years after World War III—referred to as The Water War. The film explores themes of environmentalism, technological control, and human resilience through the story of Asha, a curator at the Virtual Natural History Museum who receives a mysterious package containing soil that may indicate life still exists outside her controlled community.
entries.truth:
The film is set in the Maitu community of the East African Territory, 35 years after The Water War (World War III). This community exists as a sealed, self-sufficient environment where citizens are confined within walls due to harsh external conditions—specifically, radiation concerns and water scarcity. The word "Maitu" comes from Kikuyu roots meaning "truth" and "our," signifying "our truth" or "mother."

Asha works as the curator of the Virtual Natural History Museum, tending to digital exhibits of a world that no longer exists.
